proposals.title: Allosteric Inhibition of Human Carbonic Anhydrase IX: Synthesis and Evaluation of Dimer Interface and Proteoglycan (PG) Domain-Targeting Compounds
proposals.motivation: The canonical active site of carbonic anhydrases (CAs) has been the primary target for inhibitor design, leading to drugs like acetazolamide. However, targeting the active site can lead to off-target effects against ubiquitous CA isoforms, limiting therapeutic selectivity. Exploring non-catalytic site inhibition, such as disrupting the dimer interface critical for CA IX function or its unique PG domain, offers a novel route to achieve isoform-specific inhibition, potentially overcoming resistance and side-effect profiles of current active site-directed drugs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that small molecules or peptidomimetics designed to bind the CA IX dimer interface or the proteoglycan (PG) domain can allosterically inhibit its catalytic activity and disrupt its membrane-associated signaling functions, without directly occupying the zinc-binding active site.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of compounds, including modified coumarin and sulfonamide scaffolds known for CA affinity, but functionalized with moieties designed to interact with key residues at the CA IX dimer interface (identified via pre-2026 X-ray structures, PDB IDs: 3IAI, 5FL4). Parallel syntheses will target the PG domain. Inhibition constants (Ki) will be determined for recombinant human CA I, II, IX, and XII using stopped-flow CO2 hydration assay. Selectivity ratios (CA IX/CA II) will be calculated. Co-crystallization of lead compounds with CA IX will be attempted to confirm binding mode. Functional disruption of dimerization will be assessed via native PAGE and surface plasmon resonance (SPR).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish proof-of-concept for allosteric inhibition of CA IX, a critical cancer-associated isoform. It will provide the first series of chemical tools specifically targeting the dimer interface or PG domain, expanding the druggable landscape of the CA protein family. The findings could lead to novel therapeutic strategies with enhanced selectivity for hypoxic tumors.
proposals.novelty: While extensive work pre-2026 has focused on active site inhibitors, systematic chemical exploration of the CA IX dimer interface and PG domain as drug targets is unprecedented. This moves beyond the 'active site-centric' paradigm to validate entirely new inhibitory mechanisms.

proposals.title: Functional Interplay of the Proteoglycan (PG) Domain in Human Carbonic Anhydrase IX: Decoupling Catalytic Efficiency from Cell Adhesion for Targeted Inhibitor Design
proposals.motivation: Human carbonic anhydrase IX (CA IX) is a validated anticancer target due to its role in tumor acidification and metastasis. Its extracellular region contains a catalytic domain (CA) and a unique proteoglycan-like (PG) domain of unclear function. Pre-2026 evidence suggests the PG domain may influence both enzymatic activity and cell adhesion, but its precise mechanistic contributions remain undefined, hindering the development of domain-specific therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: The PG domain of CA IX allosterically modulates the catalytic efficiency of the CA domain and independently mediates specific protein-protein interactions crucial for cell adhesion, with these two functions being structurally and functionally separable.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of recombinant CA IX constructs: full-length extracellular domain (CA-PG), CA domain alone, and PG domain alone. Catalytic activity (kcat/KM) will be measured via stopped-flow CO2 hydration assays. Cell adhesion properties will be quantified using solid-phase binding assays with tumor cell lines and potential extracellular matrix partners. Inhibition studies will be performed using a panel of sulfonamide and coumarin-based inhibitors, including selective CA IX/XII compounds from our recent work, to probe domain-dependent inhibition profiles. Key interactions will be analyzed via molecular dynamics simulations (AMBER) focusing on the inter-domain linker region.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative dissection of the PG domain's dual role, establishing whether catalytic inhibition and anti-adhesion therapy can be independently targeted. It will deliver novel structure-activity relationship data to inform the design of next-generation bifunctional inhibitors or monoclonal antibodies that disrupt both CA IX-mediated pH regulation and metastasis.
proposals.novelty: While prior research focused on the CA domain for inhibitor design, this proposal directly investigates the under-explored PG domain as a functional and druggable entity, seeking to decouple its two suspected biological roles—a concept not yet experimentally validated in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Generation and Characterization of Monoclonal Antibodies Targeting the Dimeric Interface of Human Carbonic Anhydrase IX for Selective Anticancer Therapy
proposals.motivation: Human carbonic anhydrase IX (hCA IX) is a well-validated, hypoxia-induced tumor-associated enzyme that promotes cancer progression and metastasis by regulating pH. While small-molecule inhibitors targeting its active site have been developed, they often lack selectivity over ubiquitous off-target isoforms like hCA II, leading to side effects. The functional, dimeric form of hCA IX presents a unique, conformationally distinct epitope not present on monomeric isoforms, offering a novel avenue for highly specific targeting.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) can be generated to selectively bind the dimeric interface of hCA IX, leading to allosteric inhibition of its catalytic activity and disruption of dimer stability, with minimal cross-reactivity to monomeric CA isoforms.
proposals.method: We will immunize mice with a stabilized recombinant hCA IX dimer. Hybridoma technology will be used for mAb generation. Primary screening via ELISA will use immobilized hCA IX dimer versus monomer and hCA II to identify dimer-selective clones. Positive hits will be characterized for binding affinity (SPR), inhibition of CA activity (stopped-flow CO2 hydration assay), and ability to disrupt dimerization (native PAGE, cross-linking). The most promising mAb will be humanized and its epitope mapped via hydrogen-deuterium exchange mass spectrometry (HDX-MS) and crystallography of the Fab-dimer complex.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first proof-of-concept for a dimer-specific anti-CA IX biologic, expanding the therapeutic arsenal beyond small molecules. It will provide a novel mechanistic tool to study CA IX dimer function in tumor biology and establish a platform for developing antibody-drug conjugates or bispecific agents targeting the tumor microenvironment.
proposals.novelty: While Supuran's group has pioneered selective small-molecule inhibitors (e.g., coumarins, sulfonamides), this proposal shifts to a biologic approach targeting the quaternary structure. No pre-2026 work has reported antibodies engineered to discriminate between the monomeric and dimeric states of any CA isoform for therapeutic inhibition.

proposals.title: Synthesis, Kinetic Evaluation, and Crystallographic Analysis of 2-(Benzyl-X)-Benzoic Acid Derivatives: Probing the Essentiality of the Sulfinyl Linker for Carbonic Anhydrase Inhibition and Selectivity
proposals.motivation: The 2-(benzylsulfinyl)benzoic acid scaffold is a known class of carbonic anhydrase (CA) inhibitors, but the specific role of the sulfinyl (S=O) linker in mediating binding affinity and, crucially, selectivity among human CA isoforms remains underexplored. Given the therapeutic importance of isoform-selective CA inhibitors (e.g., targeting tumor-associated CA IX/XII over ubiquitous CA I/II), systematically challenging this linker is a rational structure-activity relationship (SAR) investigation. This work aligns with the ongoing pre-2026 research focus on developing selective inhibitors through scaffold modification.
proposals.hypothesis: The sulfinyl moiety in the 2-(benzylsulfinyl)benzoic acid scaffold is not an absolute requirement for CA inhibitory activity, and its replacement with alternative linkers (e.g., sulfonyl, methylene, ether, amide) can modulate interaction with the CA active site, potentially leading to improved selectivity profiles against disease-relevant isoforms like CA IX and XII.
proposals.method: A focused library of 2-(benzyl-X)-benzoic acid derivatives will be synthesized where X represents a series of isosteric and non-isosteric linkers: sulfonyl (SO2), methylene (CH2), ether (O), thioether (S), and amide (CONH). All compounds will be purified and characterized via standard spectroscopic methods (NMR, HRMS). Their inhibitory activity will be kinetically assessed against a panel of recombinant human CA isoforms (I, II, IX, XII) using the stopped-flow CO2 hydration assay. For the most promising compounds, X-ray crystallographic studies will be performed by co-crystallizing the inhibitors with CA II and CA IX-mimic to elucidate precise binding modes and linker interactions.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a definitive SAR map for the linker region of this benzoic acid-based chemotype, clarifying the pharmacophoric requirements. It may identify novel, selective CA IX/XII inhibitors with potential as anticancer agents or diagnostic tools. The crystallographic data will offer structural insights for the rational design of next-generation CA inhibitors with tailored linker domains.
proposals.novelty: While linker modifications are common in medicinal chemistry, a systematic, crystallography-informed investigation targeting the specific and presumed critical sulfinyl linker in this established CA inhibitor scaffold has not been comprehensively reported pre-2026. The explicit focus on testing the 'necessity' of this group for selectivity is a novel and targeted approach.

proposals.title: Elucidating the Inhibition Mechanism of Novel 4-Substituted Coumarin Derivatives on Human Carbonic Anhydrases: Distinguishing Classical Zinc-Binding from Atypical and Allosteric Modes
proposals.motivation: Recent work by our group and others has identified novel 4-substituted coumarins as potent and selective inhibitors of tumor-associated carbonic anhydrase (CA) isoforms IX and XII, with initial evidence suggesting an atypical inhibition mechanism not involving direct zinc coordination. However, the full spectrum of their inhibitory mechanisms remains ambiguous. Clarifying whether these compounds can also engage in classical zinc-binding or allosteric modulation is critical for rational drug design, as it impacts predictions of selectivity, resistance, and potential for polypharmacology within the CA enzyme family.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the novel 4-substituted coumarin derivatives, while primarily acting via an atypical mechanism, may exhibit a secondary, concentration-dependent classical zinc-binding inhibition for specific CA isoforms, or may modulate activity through binding at allosteric sites distinct from the catalytic cleft.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of the most active 4-substituted coumarins from our prior studies. Their inhibition mechanisms will be investigated using a multi-pronged approach: 1) Kinetic analysis using stopped-flow CO2 hydratase assays to determine inhibition constants (Ki) and analyze progress curves for time-dependent inhibition, a hallmark of classical sulfonamide-like binding. 2) X-ray crystallography of inhibitor complexes with human CA II (a model isoform) and target isoforms CA IX and XII to obtain high-resolution structures revealing binding site(s) and interactions with the catalytic zinc or other pockets. 3) Competitive displacement studies using fluorescent sulfonamide probes (e.g., dansylamide) to assess direct competition for the active site.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively map the mechanistic landscape of a promising class of CA inhibitors, moving beyond the simple label of 'atypical'. It will provide a structural and kinetic rationale for their isoform selectivity and inform the development of next-generation dual-mechanism inhibitors. The results will be a significant contribution to the medicinal chemistry of carbonic anhydrases, offering a template for the thorough mechanistic evaluation of new chemotypes.
proposals.novelty: While the atypical inhibition of coumarins is recognized, a systematic investigation to rule out or confirm concurrent classical zinc-binding or allosteric mechanisms in this specific, potent series of 4-substituted derivatives has not been conducted. This proposal seeks to resolve this mechanistic duality, which is non-obvious given their structural divergence from sulfonamides.

proposals.title: Synthesis and Evaluation of Novel Benzoic Acid Bioisosteres in Sulfonamide-Based Carbonic Anhydrase Inhibitors: A Strategy to Enhance Membrane Permeability and Selectivity
proposals.motivation: The benzoic acid moiety is a common feature in many potent sulfonamide carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (CAIs), but its ionized carboxylate at physiological pH can limit membrane permeability and oral bioavailability. This is a significant drawback for targeting intracellular or transmembrane CA isoforms, such as the tumor-associated hCA IX and XII. Replacing this acidic group with bioisosteres offers a rational strategy to modulate pKa, lipophilicity, and hydrogen-bonding capacity, potentially improving drug-like properties without sacrificing inhibitory potency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that replacing the benzoic acid group in known CAI scaffolds with carefully selected acidic (e.g., tetrazole, sulfonamide) or non-acidic (e.g., amide, sulfone, heterocycle) bioisosteres will yield novel compounds with retained or improved inhibitory activity against target isoforms (particularly hCA IX/XII) while significantly enhancing their physicochemical profile and cellular uptake.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of 20-30 compounds based on the coumarin-sulfonamide and aryl-sulfonamide scaffolds from our recent work, systematically replacing the benzoic acid with bioisosteres like tetrazole, isoxazole, N-acylsulfonamide, and cyclic sulfonamides. All compounds will be characterized (NMR, HRMS) and evaluated for inhibition against recombinant human CA I, II, IX, and XII using the stopped-flow CO2 hydration assay. Selectivity indices (SI) will be calculated. The most promising inhibitors will be co-crystallized with hCA II and IX for X-ray structural analysis to confirm binding modes. Their logP, pKa, and permeability in a Caco-2 cell model will be determined.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a structure-property relationship (SPR) map linking specific bioisosteric replacements to inhibitory potency, isoform selectivity, and key physicochemical parameters. It will deliver new lead compounds with optimized profiles for targeting hypoxic tumors (via hCA IX/XII) and provide a validated design strategy to decouple inhibitory activity from undesirable acidity in future CAI development.
proposals.novelty: While bioisosteric replacement is a common medicinal chemistry tactic, its systematic application to the carboxylic acid group in CAIs, particularly within the context of isoform-selective, sulfonamide-based inhibitors for anticancer applications, remains underexplored and will be guided by our unique expertise in CA crystallography and inhibitor design.

proposals.title: Systematic Chiral Resolution and Biological Profiling of Novel Carbonic Anhydrase Inhibitors: A Strategy to Uncover Enantiomer-Specific Potency and Selectivity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research in the Supuran lab and others has established that chirality significantly impacts the inhibitory potency and isoform selectivity of carbonic anhydrase (CA) inhibitors, crucial for targeting cancer-associated isoforms like hCA IX/XII. However, biological evaluation is often performed on racemic mixtures or a limited subset of separated enantiomers, obscuring the true pharmacological profile of individual stereoisomers. A systematic approach is needed to fully characterize all active chiral derivatives to identify the optimal enantiomer for therapeutic development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for each series of newly synthesized chiral CA inhibitors, one enantiomer will demonstrate significantly superior inhibitory potency and isoform selectivity (particularly against hCA IX and XII) compared to its counterpart and the racemate, and that these optimal stereoisomers can be efficiently identified through parallel chiral separation and screening.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-phase methodology. First, all active chiral derivatives from recent synthetic campaigns (including coumarin, biguanide-sulfonamide, and other chemotypes) will undergo chiral separation using analytical and preparative High-Performance Liquid Chromatography (HPLC) with polysaccharide-based chiral stationary phases (e.g., Chiralpak IA, IB, IC). Second, each purified enantiomer will be evaluated for its inhibition profile against a panel of human CA isoforms (I, II, IX, XII) using the stopped-flow CO2 hydration assay. Kinetic parameters (Ki) and selectivity ratios will be determined for each stereoisomer.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a complete stereochemical-activity relationship for multiple new classes of CA inhibitors, definitively identifying the most potent and selective enantiomers for further development. It will establish a robust, generalizable workflow for the chiral optimization of CA-targeted drugs, moving beyond the common practice of evaluating racemates. The data will be critical for rational drug design and for understanding enantioselective enzyme-inhibitor interactions.
proposals.novelty: While chiral separation of select compounds is common, a comprehensive campaign to resolve and biologically evaluate *all* active chiral derivatives from multiple synthetic series is novel. This systematic approach ensures no optimal enantiomer is overlooked and generates a complete dataset for comparative analysis across different inhibitor scaffolds.

proposals.title: In Vivo Efficacy and Toxicity Profiling of Novel 4-Substituted Coumarin Derivatives as Selective Human Carbonic Anhydrase IX Inhibitors in a Xenograft Model of Hypoxic Tumors
proposals.motivation: Human carbonic anhydrase IX (hCA IX) is a well-validated, hypoxia-inducible tumor-associated enzyme, crucial for tumor pH regulation and metastasis. Despite extensive in vitro development of selective inhibitors, such as the 4-substituted coumarins from our recent work, a critical gap exists in their in vivo validation. Demonstrating efficacy and safety in a relevant animal model is the essential next step for translating these compounds into potential anticancer therapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the most potent and selective hCA IX inhibitors from our coumarin series will significantly inhibit tumor growth and metastasis in vivo by disrupting intratumoral pH homeostasis, while exhibiting a favorable toxicity profile due to their selectivity over off-target isoforms like hCA II.
proposals.method: We will select the top two coumarin inhibitors based on prior in vitro potency (KI) and selectivity ratios (hCA IX/XII over hCA I, II). These will be formulated for intraperitoneal administration. Efficacy will be evaluated in immunodeficient mice bearing hypoxic, hCA IX-expressing HT-29 colorectal carcinoma xenografts, monitoring tumor volume, metastasis, and survival. Toxicity will be assessed via histopathology of key organs (kidney, liver) and measurement of serum biomarkers. Pharmacokinetic profiles (Cmax, T1/2) will be established to correlate exposure with effect.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive in vivo proof-of-concept for this class of coumarin-based hCA IX inhibitors, establishing a direct link between isoform-selective inhibition and antitumor activity. It will generate critical data on therapeutic window and potential organ-specific toxicities, de-risking future preclinical development and identifying a lead candidate for further optimization.
proposals.novelty: While many sulfonamide-based hCA IX inhibitors have been tested in vivo, this proposal focuses on the distinct, non-classical coumarin chemotype, which operates via a unique inhibition mechanism. The study is novel in its integrated approach to evaluating both the antitumor efficacy and the isoform-selectivity-driven safety profile of these specific compounds in a single, cohesive model.

proposals.title: Elucidating Binding Dynamics and Selectivity Profiles of Novel Coumarin and Biguanide-Sulfonamide Inhibitors for Human Carbonic Anhydrase Isoforms Using Advanced Molecular Dynamics Simulations
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the development of isoform-selective carbonic anhydrase (CA) inhibitors, particularly against cancer-associated hCA IX/XII, remains a critical challenge in medicinal chemistry. While static docking and crystallography provide snapshots of binding, they often fail to capture the dynamic interactions and solvation effects that govern inhibitor affinity and selectivity, leading to suboptimal compound optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying advanced, long-timescale molecular dynamics (MD) simulations will reveal distinct, time-dependent binding poses and stability profiles for novel coumarin and biguanide-sulfonamide derivatives, explaining their experimentally observed selectivity for hCA IX/XII over off-target isoforms like hCA I/II.
proposals.method: We will perform all-atom, explicit-solvent MD simulations using the AMBER or CHARMM force fields on the most active compounds from recent syntheses (e.g., 4-substituted coumarins, biguanide-aryl sulfonamides). Systems will be built from available X-ray structures (e.g., PDB IDs for hCA II, IX, XII). Simulations will be extended to the microsecond timescale using GPU-accelerated platforms (e.g., AMBER's pmemd.cuda) to ensure convergence. Binding free energies will be calculated via the Molecular Mechanics/Generalized Born Surface Area (MM/GBSA) method, and interaction fingerprints will be analyzed over time to identify key dynamic residues.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a dynamic, atomic-resolution rationale for the selectivity of newly synthesized inhibitors, moving beyond static structural models. It will generate predictive insights into inhibitor-CA interactions to guide the rational design of next-generation compounds with improved potency and isoform specificity.
proposals.novelty: While MD is established, its application to these specific, newly synthesized chemotypes (coumarins, biguanide-sulfonamides) for CA inhibition is limited. The novelty lies in the systematic, comparative microsecond-scale dynamics analysis across multiple human CA isoforms to decode selectivity, a direct extension of Supuran's recent experimental findings.

proposals.title: Fragment-Based Drug Design and Structural Optimization of 4-Substituted Coumarin Derivatives for Selective Inhibition of Human Carbonic Anhydrase IX and XII
proposals.motivation: The tumor-associated carbonic anhydrase isoforms hCA IX and XII are validated anti-cancer targets, but achieving high selectivity over off-target cytosolic isoforms remains a significant challenge. The 4-substituted coumarin scaffold, recently explored by Supuran's group, shows promising selectivity but requires systematic optimization of its substituents to enhance potency and drug-like properties. Fragment-based drug design (FBDD) offers a rational, structure-guided approach to this optimization problem that has not yet been applied to this specific chemotype.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that fragment screening against hCA IX and XII will identify optimal chemical moieties that, when strategically linked or grown from the 4-position of the coumarin core, will yield novel derivatives with significantly improved inhibitory potency and isoform selectivity compared to existing leads.
proposals.method: We will employ a biophysical fragment screen (thermal shift assay, surface plasmon resonance) against recombinant hCA IX and XII using a diverse library of 500-1000 small fragments. Hits will be characterized by X-ray crystallography in complex with the target isoforms to determine precise binding modes. Computational fragment linking and growing will be performed using structure-based design software (e.g., Schrödinger's Core Hopping, MOE's Fragment Builder) guided by the co-crystal structures. The most promising in silico designs will be synthesized, followed by rigorous enzymatic inhibition assays against a panel of hCA isoforms (I, II, IX, XII) and cytotoxicity studies in relevant cancer cell lines.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new series of highly selective hCA IX/XII inhibitors with characterized binding modes, providing valuable chemical tools for oncology research. It will establish a validated FBDD pipeline for the coumarin scaffold, a strategy applicable to other CA inhibitor chemotypes. The project will generate high-resolution structural data on fragment binding, elucidating novel subpocket interactions within the CA active site.
proposals.novelty: While FBDD is established in drug discovery, its application to the 4-substituted coumarin CA inhibitor scaffold is novel. The approach moves beyond traditional analogue synthesis by using experimental fragment hits to rationally inform substituent design, a significant departure from the group's recent exploration of pre-defined substituents.

proposals.title: Development of Scalable Synthetic Routes for Preclinical Advancement of Selective Human Carbonic Anhydrase IX/XII Inhibitors
proposals.motivation: The Supuran group has identified several potent and selective inhibitors of cancer-associated human carbonic anhydrase isoforms (hCA IX and XII), such as novel 4-substituted coumarins and biguanide-containing sulfonamides. However, their synthesis is typically limited to small-scale, multi-step laboratory procedures unsuitable for producing the multi-gram quantities required for rigorous preclinical formulation, pharmacokinetic, and toxicology studies. This bottleneck impedes the translation of these promising compounds into viable drug candidates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically redesigning and optimizing the synthetic pathways for our lead hCA IX/XII inhibitors, we can develop robust, cost-effective, and scalable (multi-gram) routes that maintain high chemical purity and yield, enabling comprehensive preclinical development.
proposals.method: We will first select two lead candidates from our recent work: a 4-substituted coumarin and a biguanide-aryl sulfonamide. For each, we will perform retrosynthetic analysis to identify bottlenecks and replace costly/reagents or low-yielding steps. Key strategies will include: 1) transitioning from chromatographic purification to crystallization or distillation; 2) employing flow chemistry for exothermic or hazardous steps; 3) identifying and sourcing cheaper, scalable building blocks; and 4) optimizing reaction conditions (solvent, catalyst, temperature) for larger volumes. Process analytical technology (PAT) will be used for in-line monitoring. The final scaled routes (target: 10-20g) will be validated over three consecutive batches.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first reported scalable syntheses for these novel classes of hCA inhibitors, delivering the material necessary for advanced preclinical studies, including nanoparticle formulation (as explored in our group's recent work) and ADMET profiling. It will establish a practical framework for transitioning from medicinal chemistry hits to development candidates within the carbonic anhydrase inhibitor field.
proposals.novelty: While synthetic methods for these inhibitors exist, the systematic development and publication of industrially viable, scaled-up routes specifically tailored for preclinical advancement of hCA IX/XII inhibitors is novel and addresses a critical gap in the drug discovery pipeline for these targets.

proposals.title: Deconvoluting Synergistic CA Inhibition: A Phenotypic Screening and Fractionation Approach to Identify Active Components from Complex Natural Extracts
proposals.motivation: Traditional drug discovery against carbonic anhydrase (CA) isoforms relies on pure, isolated compounds, a process that is time-consuming, costly, and may overlook synergistic effects present in natural mixtures. Pre-2026, there is a growing interest in polypharmacology and the therapeutic potential of multi-component systems, especially for targeting complex diseases like cancer where multiple CA isoforms (e.g., CA IX/XII) are involved. Developing methods to validate and characterize bioactive mixtures without full isolation could accelerate the discovery of novel CA inhibitors and combination therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: That the inhibitory activity of selected natural extracts against specific human CA isoforms (particularly tumor-associated CA IX and XII) is attributable to identifiable, synergistic combinations of compounds within the mixture, which can be characterized and standardized without requiring the isolation of every individual component to purity.
proposals.method: We will employ a bioassay-guided fractionation strategy coupled with advanced analytical techniques. Crude plant/fungal extracts showing potent CA inhibition in initial phenotypic screens will be fractionated via semi-preparative HPLC. The inhibitory activity of each fraction will be tested against recombinant human CA I, II, IX, and XII. Active fractions will be analyzed by LC-MS/MS and NMR spectroscopy. Data will be integrated using multivariate analysis (e.g., Principal Component Analysis) and molecular networking (via Global Natural Products Social Molecular Networking, GNPS) to correlate spectral features with bioactivity, identifying key clusters of compounds responsible for the observed effect.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a validated pipeline for the discovery of CA inhibitors from complex mixtures, moving beyond the pure compound paradigm. It will provide novel, potentially synergistic, multi-component leads targeting cancer-associated CA isoforms. The methodology will contribute to the field of natural product drug discovery by demonstrating how to define and standardize bioactive mixtures for further development.
proposals.novelty: While Supuran's group has extensively synthesized and tested pure compounds, this proposal directly challenges that isolation imperative by systematically deconvoluting mixture activity. The novelty lies in applying modern metabolomics and chemoinformatic tools (GNPS, multivariate analysis) specifically to CA inhibition to identify synergistic compound families, rather than single entities, as potential drug candidates.

proposals.title: Systematic Re-evaluation of Natural Product Scaffolds as Inhibitors of Pathogen-Associated and Human Carbonic Anhydrases
proposals.motivation: The search for novel carbonic anhydrase (CA) inhibitors is heavily focused on synthesizing new chemical entities, often overlooking the vast, pre-existing chemical space of natural products (NPs). Many NP scaffolds have known bioactivity but have not been systematically profiled against the expanding family of human and pathogenic CAs. Re-investigating these compounds, particularly against isoforms implicated in diseases like cancer (CA IX/XII) and bacterial infections (e.g., E. coli CA), offers a rapid, resource-efficient path to discovering new biological activities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that known natural product scaffolds, previously characterized for other biological activities, possess untapped and selective inhibitory potential against specific human (e.g., tumor-associated CA IX/XII) and pathogen-derived carbonic anhydrase isoforms.
proposals.method: We will curate a focused library of 150-200 commercially available natural products and their semi-synthetic derivatives, prioritizing scaffolds with structural motifs (e.g., coumarins, polyphenols, sulfonamide analogs) known to interact with CA active sites. Inhibition constants (Ki) will be determined using a stopped-flow CO2 hydration assay against a panel of recombinant human (hCA I, II, IX, XII) and bacterial (E. coli β-CA) isoforms. For promising hits, X-ray crystallography of inhibitor-CA complexes will be performed to elucidate binding modes, guiding minimal structural optimization via synthetic modification of the core NP scaffold to enhance potency and selectivity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new structure-activity relationship (SAR) database linking natural product chemotypes to inhibition profiles across clinically relevant CA isoforms. It will identify new lead compounds for anti-infective or anticancer applications without de novo synthesis and provide high-resolution crystal structures of NP-CA complexes, revealing novel binding interactions.
proposals.novelty: While NPs are known CA inhibitors, a systematic, isoform-selective profiling campaign against both human disease-associated and pathogenic bacterial CAs is lacking. The novelty lies in deliberately relaxing scaffold novelty to instead discover novel biological activities from existing, pharmacologically privileged structures, leveraging modern kinetic and structural methods.

proposals.title: Exploiting Natural Product Scaffolds for Multi-Isoform Carbonic Anhydrase Inhibition: Synthesis, Profiling, and Therapeutic Potential in Complex Pathologies
proposals.motivation: The dominant drug discovery paradigm for carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (CAIs) has focused on achieving high selectivity for single isoforms (e.g., CA IX/XII for cancer). However, many diseases, including infections and complex metabolic syndromes, involve dysregulation of multiple CA isoforms across different tissues and cell types. Pre-2026, the inherent polypharmacology of natural products is increasingly recognized not as a liability but as a potential advantage for treating multifactorial diseases, yet this concept remains underexplored in the CA field.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rationally designed derivatives based on natural product scaffolds with inherent promiscuity (e.g., coumarins, polyphenols) can be synthesized to potently and simultaneously inhibit a therapeutically relevant spectrum of human and bacterial carbonic anhydrase isoforms, leading to superior efficacy in models of complex disease compared to single-target inhibitors.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of derivatives based on coumarin and phenolic acid scaffolds, incorporating sulfonamide or sulfocoumarin warheads to enhance CA binding. Inhibition constants (Ki) will be determined for a panel of human (CA I, II, IX, XII) and bacterial (e.g., E. coli CAs) isoforms using stopped-flow CO2 hydratase assays. The most promising multi-target agents will be co-crystallized with key isoforms for X-ray structural analysis to elucidate binding modes. Their efficacy will be evaluated in cellular models of bacterial biofilm formation (targeting E. coli CAs) and tumor cell acidification (targeting CA IX/XII/II).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel library of multi-target CAIs with defined isoform inhibition profiles, challenging the single-isoform selectivity dogma. It will establish a proof-of-concept that deliberate polypharmacology against CAs can be a viable strategy for complex conditions like persistent infections or tumor microenvironments, potentially overcoming compensatory mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While Supuran's group has developed selective CA inhibitors from coumarins, this proposal deliberately reverses the objective to engineer and optimize *multi-isoform* inhibition profiles from natural scaffolds, integrating targeting of human and bacterial CAs into single chemical entities for a synergistic therapeutic effect.

proposals.title: Predictive ADMET and Property Modeling for Natural Product-Derived Carbonic Anhydrase Inhibitors: A Cheminformatics Approach to Bridge the Synthetic-Natural Compound Divide
proposals.motivation: Natural products are a rich source of novel carbonic anhydrase (CA) inhibitor scaffolds, but their development is often hindered by unpredictable pharmacokinetics and toxicity. Pre-2026, advanced cheminformatic models for ADMET (Absorption, Distribution, Metabolism, Excretion, Toxicity) and physicochemical properties have been developed and validated primarily on large libraries of synthetic compounds, creating a potential bias. Applying these robust, data-driven models to natural product-like chemical space could accelerate the identification of viable CA-targeting drug candidates from natural sources.
proposals.hypothesis: Predictive models (e.g., for solubility, permeability, metabolic stability, and hERG inhibition) trained on synthetic compound libraries can be reliably applied to natural product-derived CA inhibitors to prioritize leads with favorable drug-like properties, thereby streamlining the early development pipeline.
proposals.method: We will curate a dataset of natural products and their semi-synthetic derivatives known to exhibit CA inhibition (from literature up to 2025). Using established pre-2026 software (e.g., RDKit, Schrödinger's QikProp, or OpenADMET), we will compute a panel of molecular descriptors and apply pre-trained machine learning models (e.g., Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, or Graph Neural Networks as implemented in DeepChem) to predict key ADMET endpoints. The computational predictions will be validated against experimental data for a subset of compounds synthesized in-house, focusing on CA IX/XII isoforms, with correlation analysis performed to assess model transferability.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated computational framework for the pre-screening of natural product-based CA inhibitors, reducing costly late-stage attrition. It will generate a publicly available dataset correlating predicted and experimental properties for natural product-like CA inhibitors, filling a critical gap in cheminformatic resources. The study will also identify which ADMET endpoints are most reliably predicted across the synthetic-natural chemical divide.
proposals.novelty: While cheminformatic models are widely used for synthetic libraries, their systematic application and validation specifically for natural product-derived CA inhibitors is unexplored. This proposal directly integrates Supuran's expertise in CA inhibitor synthesis and evaluation with modern predictive toxicology and property modeling, creating a novel intersection between natural product chemistry and data-driven drug design.

proposals.title: Synthesis and Evaluation of Novel Sulfonamide-Biguanide Hybrids with Tailored Physicochemical Properties for Enhanced Pharmacokinetics and Tumor-Targeted Carbonic Anhydrase Inhibition
proposals.motivation: Despite the potent inhibition of tumor-associated carbonic anhydrase isoforms (CA IX/XII) by many sulfonamide derivatives, their clinical translation is often hampered by suboptimal pharmacokinetics, including poor aqueous solubility and limited tissue penetration. Improving these drug-like properties is critical for enhancing bioavailability and therapeutic efficacy in cancer therapy, a major pre-2026 challenge in medicinal chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strategically conjugating a sulfonamide pharmacophore with a biguanide moiety, known for its favorable physicochemical and pharmacokinetic profile, will yield novel hybrid inhibitors with enhanced solubility, membrane permeability, and sustained plasma exposure, while retaining potent and selective inhibition of CA IX/XII.
proposals.method: A focused library of 15-20 sulfonamide-biguanide hybrids will be synthesized via established coupling reactions. Their inhibitory potency and selectivity against recombinant human CA I, II, IX, and XII will be determined using a stopped-flow CO2 hydration assay. Key ADME properties (aqueous solubility, logP, plasma protein binding, metabolic stability in human liver microsomes) will be measured experimentally. The most promising candidate will be co-crystallized with CA IX to obtain high-resolution X-ray structures, elucidating binding interactions and guiding further optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new series of CA inhibitors with a comprehensively characterized ADME profile, directly addressing the solubility-penetration bottleneck. It will establish structure-property relationship (SPR) guidelines for optimizing CA inhibitor pharmacokinetics and provide a high-resolution structural template for future rational design of tumor-targeted therapies.
proposals.novelty: While biguanides and sulfonamides have been studied separately, their systematic hybridization to specifically engineer pharmacokinetic advantages for CA inhibition is novel. This approach integrates rational medicinal chemistry design with comprehensive ADME profiling and structural biology, moving beyond pure potency optimization.

proposals.title: De Novo Design of Miniprotein Inhibitors Targeting Pathogenic Human Carbonic Anhydrase Isoforms IX and XII
proposals.motivation: Selective inhibition of cancer-associated human carbonic anhydrase (hCA) isoforms IX and XII remains a key therapeutic goal, as current small-molecule inhibitors often lack sufficient isoform selectivity, leading to off-target effects. De novo protein design offers a route to create entirely new, high-affinity protein-based scaffolds that can achieve exquisite selectivity by precisely matching the target's surface topology, a level of control difficult to attain with traditional organic chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that computational de novo protein design can generate novel, stable miniprotein scaffolds that bind with high affinity and selectivity to the catalytic domains of hCA IX and XII, surpassing the selectivity profiles of current small-molecule sulfonamides and coumarins.
proposals.method: We will employ the RosettaDesign suite (pre-2026) to generate de novo miniprotein binders. Starting from the crystal structures of hCA IX and XII, we will use the RosettaRemodel protocol to design complementary binding interfaces. The RFdiffusion algorithm (as of 2025) will be used to generate novel backbone scaffolds conditioned on target binding sites. Designed sequences will be filtered for stability (Rosetta energy scores, AlphaFold2 predicted local distance difference test (pLDDT)), synthesized, and expressed in E. coli. Binding affinity (Kd) will be measured by surface plasmon resonance, and inhibitory activity (Ki) by stopped-flow CO2 hydration assay, with selectivity assessed against off-target isoforms (e.g., hCA I, II).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first computationally designed protein-based inhibitors for hCA isoforms, establishing a new class of CA modulators. It will provide a proof-of-concept for achieving extreme isoform selectivity via designed protein interfaces, opening a new avenue for therapeutic and diagnostic applications in oncology and beyond.
proposals.novelty: While computational design has been used for enzyme engineering, its application to create de novo protein inhibitors of carbonic anhydrases is unprecedented. This moves beyond the modification of existing scaffolds (e.g., antibodies) or small molecules, representing a paradigm shift in CA inhibitor design.

proposals.title: High-Throughput Discovery of Novel Carbonic Anhydrase Inhibitors via DNA-Encoded Chemical Library Screening Against Multiple Human Isoforms
proposals.motivation: The development of isoform-selective carbonic anhydrase (CA) inhibitors remains a critical challenge in designing targeted therapies for cancer (CA IX/XII) and other diseases. Traditional high-throughput screening (HTS) and rational design approaches are limited in chemical space exploration, often yielding compounds with insufficient selectivity or potency. DNA-encoded library (DEL) technology offers an unprecedented opportunity to screen billions of compounds simultaneously, but its application to the CA field, particularly for discovering non-classical chemotypes beyond sulfonamides, is underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that screening ultra-large DNA-encoded chemical libraries against immobilized human CA isoforms (including CA I, II, IX, and XII) will rapidly identify novel, high-affinity chemotypes with improved selectivity profiles compared to current aryl sulfonamide-based inhibitors.
proposals.method: We will synthesize or procure DELs containing billions of compounds, incorporating diverse building blocks including coumarin, sulfonamide, and biguanide derivatives. Recombinant human CA isoforms (hCA I, II, IX, XII) will be individually immobilized on solid supports via His-tag capture. Parallel affinity selection campaigns will be performed for each isoform under controlled pH and buffer conditions. After selection, bound compounds will be eluted, and the associated DNA barcodes will be amplified and sequenced using next-generation sequencing (NGS). Hit compounds will be decoded, re-synthesized off-DNA, and validated via standard stopped-flow CO2 hydrase assay and X-ray crystallography for binding mode determination.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new set of validated, potent CA inhibitors with defined isoform selectivity, expanding the known chemical matter for CA inhibition. It will establish a robust DEL screening platform for CA isoforms, enabling future rapid discovery campaigns. The project will also provide structural insights into novel binding modes, informing subsequent rational drug design.
proposals.novelty: While DEL technology is established, its systematic application to the parallel discovery of inhibitors for multiple human CA isoforms, with direct integration of kinetic validation and crystallography from the Supuran lab's expertise, represents a novel and powerful synergy. This approach moves beyond screening single targets or focused libraries to a proteome-family-wide discovery strategy.

proposals.title: Rational Engineering of Human Carbonic Anhydrase II and IX for Selective Inhibitor Resistance: A Reverse-Design Strategy to Decipher Binding Determinants
proposals.motivation: The clinical utility of carbonic anhydrase (CA) inhibitors, particularly against cancer-associated isoforms like CA IX, is often limited by off-target inhibition of ubiquitous isoforms like CA II, leading to side effects. Understanding the precise structural and energetic determinants of inhibitor binding is crucial for designing isoform-selective drugs. An inverse approach—systematically engineering resistance into specific CA isoforms—provides a direct, functional probe of these binding determinants, complementing traditional inhibitor design.
proposals.hypothesis: Targeted mutations in the active site of human CA II and CA IX, guided by computational analysis of inhibitor-enzyme complexes, will confer selective resistance to specific inhibitor chemotypes (e.g., coumarins, sulfonamides, biguanides), revealing key residues and interactions responsible for binding affinity and selectivity.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of mutant CA II and CA IX constructs based on in silico saturation mutagenesis and free energy perturbation (FEP) calculations using Schrödinger's Maestro suite, focusing on active site residues (e.g., His94, Val121, Leu198). Mutant proteins will be expressed, purified, and characterized via kinetic assays (stopped-flow CO2 hydration) to determine catalytic efficiency. Inhibitor resistance profiles will be established by measuring Ki values against a panel of pre-2026 inhibitors (sulfonamides, coumarins, biguanides) from our recent work. Key mutants will be analyzed by X-ray crystallography to validate structural predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a novel map of 'resistance-conferring' mutations for major CA inhibitor classes, directly linking specific residues to binding energy contributions. It will provide a validated reverse-design framework for understanding selectivity, informing the development of next-generation inhibitors with reduced off-target effects. The engineered, resistant CA variants may also serve as unique tools for cellular studies.
proposals.novelty: While most studies focus on designing inhibitors against wild-type CAs, this proposal inverts the problem by engineering the enzyme target itself to be resistant, a direct functional genetics approach not commonly applied to human CA isoforms. It systematically correlates computational predictions of resistance with experimental kinetic and structural validation across multiple inhibitor chemotypes.

proposals.title: Development of a Phage-Display-Derived Peptidic Inhibitor with Nanomolar Affinity and High Specificity for Tumor-Associated Carbonic Anhydrase IX
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, small-molecule sulfonamide inhibitors of carbonic anhydrases (CAs) often lack absolute isoform selectivity, leading to off-target effects against ubiquitous isoforms like CA I and II. This is a critical barrier for therapeutic applications, especially in oncology targeting CA IX, where exquisite specificity is required to avoid systemic toxicity. Biologic inhibitors, such as engineered peptides, offer a promising but underexplored route to achieve this extreme selectivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a peptide library screened against the unique extracellular domain of human CA IX will yield binders that, when engineered and optimized, can function as potent and highly selective non-classical inhibitors of CA IX activity, with minimal cross-reactivity to other CA isoforms.
proposals.method: A phage-displayed cyclic peptide library (10^9 diversity) will be panned against recombinant human CA IX protein, with counter-selection against CA II and CA XII to drive specificity. High-affinity binders will be identified by NGS of enriched phage pools and validated by SPR for binding kinetics (KD). Lead peptide sequences will be synthesized, conjugated to a sulfonamide zinc-binding group via a flexible linker, and tested for CA inhibition using stopped-flow CO2 hydration assay. Co-crystallization with CA IX and molecular dynamics simulations will guide iterative optimization of linker length and peptide structure.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first rationally designed peptide-sulfonamide hybrid inhibitor for a CA isoform, establishing a novel class of CA-targeting biologics. It will provide a structural blueprint for achieving extreme isoform selectivity beyond what is possible with traditional small molecules, opening new avenues for diagnostic and therapeutic applications in hypoxic tumors.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Supuran's group has extensively explored novel small-molecule chemotypes (e.g., coumarins, biguanides), this proposal pivots to a biologic-peptide-based inhibition strategy, a radical departure from his established research. The fusion of a phage-derived targeting peptide with a classical zinc-binding group is a novel hybrid approach not reported in the CA field pre-2026.

proposals.title: Synthesis and Evaluation of Hypoxia-Responsive Prodrugs Targeting Carbonic Anhydrase IX for Selective Tumor Therapy
proposals.motivation: Current CA inhibitors, particularly for cancer-relevant isoforms like hCA IX, often lack sufficient selectivity, leading to off-target effects against ubiquitous isoforms like hCA II. The perpetual inhibition of these enzymes can disrupt normal physiological pH regulation. A strategy for conditional activation of inhibitors specifically in the tumor microenvironment (TME), characterized by hypoxia and acidosis, is needed to improve therapeutic windows and reduce side effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that masking the sulfonamide zinc-binding group of known hCA IX inhibitors with a hypoxia-labile nitroaromatic trigger will yield prodrugs that remain inert towards off-target CAs under normoxic conditions but are selectively activated to potent inhibitors within hypoxic tumor tissues.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of prodrugs by conjugating established hCA IX/XII-selective sulfonamide scaffolds (e.g., coumarin or benzenesulfonamide cores from recent work) to a 4-nitrobenzyl moiety via a carbamate or carbonate linker. The inhibitory activity (Ki) of the prodrugs and their activated forms will be determined against recombinant hCA I, II, IX, and XII using a stopped-flow CO2 hydrase assay. Activation kinetics under simulated hypoxia (using azoreductase or sodium dithionite) and selectivity will be assessed in cell cultures of HT-29 (high hCA IX) under normoxic vs. hypoxic conditions. X-ray crystallography of key prodrug-CA complexes will elucidate binding modes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel class of 'smart' CA inhibitors with an on-off mechanism controlled by the TME. It will provide proof-of-concept for transient, conditionally active CA therapy, potentially improving the safety profile of CA-targeted anticancer agents. The findings will advance the field from perpetual inhibition towards spatially and temporally controlled pharmacological intervention.
proposals.novelty: While prodrug strategies exist, their application to CA inhibitors has been limited, and a deliberate design for hypoxia-triggered activation of the zinc-binding sulfonamide group is unexplored. This moves beyond simple isoform-selective design to introduce an environmental-response layer, a significant conceptual shift in CA inhibitor development.

proposals.title: Systematic Identification of Endogenous Metabolites as Selective Substrates for Human Carbonic Anhydrase Isoforms: A Reverse-Engineering Approach for Inhibitor Inspiration
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the design of isoform-selective carbonic anhydrase (CA) inhibitors has largely focused on synthetic sulfonamides and coumarins, often targeting the catalytic zinc or its vicinity. While successful, this approach can be limited by off-target effects. The natural substrates and modulators that differentiate the 15 human CA isoforms in vivo are poorly characterized. Identifying such endogenous selective binders would provide a novel, biologically validated chemical blueprint for designing next-generation inhibitors with improved selectivity and therapeutic profiles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific endogenous metabolites, beyond the canonical substrate CO2/HCO3-, act as selective substrates or modulators for distinct human CA isoforms, and that their binding modes can be reverse-engineered to inform the design of novel, highly selective CA inhibitors.
proposals.method: We will employ an integrated computational and experimental pipeline. First, a virtual screening of a pre-2026 library of human endogenous metabolites (e.g., from HMDB) against the X-ray structures of CA I, II, IX, and XII will be performed using molecular docking (AutoDock Vina, Glide). Top candidates will be filtered by molecular dynamics (GROMACS) for binding stability. Selected metabolites will then be synthesized or sourced and tested kinetically for CA isoform-specific catalytic activity or inhibition using stopped-flow CO2 hydration assays. Co-crystallization of isoform-metabolite complexes will be attempted to obtain definitive structural insights.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate a novel map of endogenous metabolite interactions across key CA isoforms, identifying new starting points for inhibitor design. It will contribute fundamental knowledge on CA biology beyond its role in gas hydration. The project is expected to yield high-resolution structures of CA isoforms with novel endogenous ligands, providing direct templates for structure-based drug design.
proposals.novelty: This approach inverts the traditional drug discovery paradigm by seeking natural selectivity clues from endogenous substrates rather than optimizing synthetic inhibitors. It systematically applies pre-2026 structural bioinformatics and kinetic methods to a largely unexplored question of CA isoform differentiation by native metabolites.

proposals.title: A Theranostic Trial Design for Patient Stratification in Clinical Evaluation of Novel Carbonic Anhydrase IX Inhibitors in Breast Cancer
proposals.motivation: Despite the well-established role of tumor-associated carbonic anhydrase IX (CAIX) in promoting tumor acidosis, invasion, and metastasis in breast cancer, clinical trials for CAIX inhibitors have shown limited success, partly due to heterogeneous target expression across patients. This leads to the inclusion of non-responders, diluting efficacy signals and hindering drug approval. A pre-2026 strategy to enrich trial populations for target-positive patients is urgently needed to demonstrate therapeutic potential.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a theranostic trial design, where patients are first screened for high CAIX expression using a validated PET imaging agent, will enrich the trial population and significantly increase the observed clinical efficacy of novel, selective CAIX inhibitors in metastatic breast cancer.
proposals.method: We will synthesize and validate a CAIX-targeted PET tracer, such as a 68Ga-labeled derivative of a high-affinity sulfonamide inhibitor (e.g., from the 4-substituted coumarin series) or a 89Zr-labeled anti-CAIX monoclonal antibody fragment. In a Phase 0/II trial, patients with triple-negative breast cancer will undergo PET/CT imaging. Only those with a predefined threshold of tumor uptake (SUVmax) will be enrolled in the subsequent therapeutic arm, where they will receive a novel, potent CAIX inhibitor (e.g., a biguanide-containing aryl sulfonamide or a coumarin derivative). Primary endpoints will include objective response rate and progression-free survival, compared to historical controls from unselected populations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated clinical protocol for patient stratification in CAIX-targeted therapy, directly increasing the probability of a successful Phase II trial. It will establish a crucial link between CAIX expression levels measured in vivo and therapeutic response, creating a precision medicine framework for this target class. The project will also deliver a clinically usable CAIX-PET imaging agent.
proposals.novelty: While CAIX PET tracers and inhibitors have been developed independently, their systematic integration into a single, target-enriching clinical trial design for breast cancer, directly adapted from successful nuclear medicine paradigms (e.g., PSMA-targeted therapy), is a novel and untested translational strategy.

proposals.title: Real-Time Visualization of CAIX Inhibitor-Induced Metabolic Stress in Breast Cancer Using FRET-Based Lipid Biosensors
proposals.motivation: Carbonic anhydrase IX (CAIX) is a validated, hypoxia-induced therapeutic target in aggressive breast cancers, but clinical responses to its inhibitors are variable. A major pre-2026 challenge is the lack of direct, real-time pharmacodynamic biomarkers to monitor drug action and predict therapeutic efficacy in live tumor models, hindering optimal patient stratification and dosing. Linking specific CAIX inhibition to downstream metabolic perturbations, particularly in trans-Golgi network (TGN) lipid signaling, remains unexplored in a dynamic, live-cell context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that novel, selective CAIX inhibitors will induce acute metabolic stress in breast cancer cells, manifesting as specific, quantifiable alterations in diacylglycerol (DAG) and sphingolipid dynamics at the TGN, and that these real-time biosensor readouts will correlate directly with subsequent therapeutic response and cell death.
proposals.method: We will synthesize novel sulfonamide or coumarin-based CAIX inhibitors, building on Supuran's expertise in isoform-selective compound design. These will be applied to patient-derived breast cancer organoids and xenograft models expressing genetically encoded FRET biosensors for DAG (e.g., C1 domain-based) and sphingolipids (e.g., CERT PH domain-based) targeted to the TGN. Live-cell confocal imaging will quantify FRET ratio changes upon inhibitor treatment, correlating these kinetic 'lipid flares' with orthogonal measures of cell viability (e.g., caspase-3 activation, clonogenic assays) and extracellular acidification rates (Seahorse analysis).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel functional imaging platform that directly links CAIX pharmacological inhibition to its immediate metabolic consequences in live tumors. It will provide the first real-time, mechanistic pharmacodynamic readout for CAIX-targeted therapy, moving beyond static endpoint analyses. The findings will deliver candidate predictive biomarkers (specific lipid flux signatures) to guide future clinical trial design for metabolic cancer therapies.
proposals.novelty: The integration of dynamic, compartment-specific lipid biosensor imaging with the evaluation of novel CAIX inhibitors in patient-derived models is unprecedented. This approach transforms CAIX inhibition from a static biochemical event into a visualized metabolic process, offering a non-obvious, functional method for therapy optimization and resistance monitoring.

proposals.title: Sequential Targeting of Hypoxic Tumors: A CAIX Inhibitor Pre-treatment Strategy to Sensitize Triple-Negative Breast Cancer to Paclitaxel
proposals.motivation: The acidic, hypoxic tumor microenvironment (TME) driven by carbonic anhydrase IX (CAIX) promotes chemoresistance and metastasis, limiting the efficacy of standard therapies. Pre-2026, CAIX inhibitors are primarily studied in concurrent combination with chemotherapy, but the critical importance of temporal sequence, inspired by successful antimicrobial biofilm eradication protocols, remains unexplored for optimizing therapeutic impact.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a defined pre-treatment with a selective CAIX inhibitor will normalize the TME and compromise cancer cell adaptations, thereby significantly enhancing the cytotoxic and anti-metastatic effects of subsequently administered paclitaxel compared to simultaneous administration.
proposals.method: We will synthesize novel 4-substituted coumarin sulfonamides with high selectivity for CAIX/XII, confirmed by kinetic inhibition assays against recombinant human isoforms. Using 3D spheroid models of triple-negative breast cancer (MDA-MB-231), we will establish an optimized sequential protocol: pre-incubation with the lead CAIX inhibitor (24-48h) followed by paclitaxel treatment. Efficacy will be assessed via spheroid growth inhibition, apoptosis (caspase-3/7), extracellular pH monitoring, and markers of hypoxia (HIF-1α) and metastasis (MMPs). The optimal sequence will be validated in a murine xenograft model.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, rationale-driven sequential treatment paradigm for hypoxic tumors, moving beyond empirical combination. It will provide a proof-of-concept that temporal optimization, akin to antimicrobial strategies, can maximize therapeutic efficacy and potentially overcome chemoresistance mechanisms rooted in the TME.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translational of a temporal treatment model from infectious disease (biofilm disruption) to oncology, and the systematic investigation of sequence-dependent efficacy rather than simple concurrent CAIX inhibition, which is a significant departure from existing pre-2026 research.

proposals.title: Synthesis and Preclinical Evaluation of a CAIX-Targeted, MMP-Activatable Fluorescent Probe for High-Contrast Imaging of Tumor Hypoxia
proposals.motivation: Tumor hypoxia, driven by overexpression of carbonic anhydrase IX (CAIX), is a major therapeutic challenge associated with metastasis, treatment resistance, and poor prognosis. Current CAIX-targeted imaging probes, often 'always-on' fluorescent sulfonamides, suffer from high background signal, limiting their sensitivity and specificity for precise tumor delineation and treatment monitoring in vivo.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a CAIX-targeted molecular probe, designed to be optically silent until activated by matrix metalloproteinase-2/9 (MMP-2/9) in the hypoxic tumor microenvironment, will provide a significantly higher signal-to-noise ratio for non-invasive hypoxia imaging compared to conventional always-on probes.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a novel probe by conjugating a high-affinity CAIX inhibitor (a selective coumarin or sulfonamide derivative, based on our recent work) to a near-infrared fluorophore (e.g., Cy5.5) via a cleavable peptide linker specific for MMP-2/9, with a complementary quencher molecule attached. The probe's binding affinity and inhibitory potency for CAIX will be determined using stopped-flow kinetics and X-ray crystallography. Activation kinetics and specificity will be tested in vitro with recombinant MMPs. Finally, in vivo imaging performance, biodistribution, and contrast will be evaluated in mouse xenograft models of hypoxic tumors (e.g., HT-29 colorectal carcinoma) using fluorescence molecular tomography.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a first-in-class activatable, dual-targeted probe for CAIX, providing a powerful tool for high-contrast, non-invasive visualization of hypoxic tumor niches. It will advance the field of molecular imaging by translating an activatable probe strategy from neuroscience to oncology, potentially enabling more sensitive detection of micrometastases and earlier assessment of therapeutic response.
proposals.novelty: While CAIX inhibitors and activatable (smart) probes exist separately, their integration into a single, CAIX-anchored, protease-activated construct for hypoxia imaging is novel. This approach uniquely leverages the co-expression of CAIX and MMPs in the hypoxic niche to achieve spatially confined signal activation.

proposals.title: Large-Scale In Vitro Evolution and Mechanistic Profiling of Acquired Resistance to Selective Human Carbonic Anhydrase IX Inhibitors
proposals.motivation: The tumor-associated, hypoxia-inducible isoform CAIX is a validated anticancer target, with many potent and selective inhibitors developed, including coumarin and sulfonamide derivatives. However, as with most targeted therapies, the potential for acquired resistance remains a critical, underexplored risk that could limit long-term clinical efficacy. Pre-emptively identifying resistance mechanisms in vitro is essential for designing next-generation inhibitors and rational combination therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that prolonged selective pressure from CAIX-targeted inhibitors will drive the evolution of resistant cancer cell populations through specific, reproducible genetic and phenotypic adaptations, including mutations in the CA9 gene, altered CAIX expression, or activation of compensatory pH-regulatory pathways.
proposals.method: We will employ a large-scale, parallel in vitro evolution screen using HT-1080 fibrosarcoma cells stably expressing CAIX. Cells will be subjected to increasing sub-lethal concentrations of novel 4-substituted coumarins and biguanide-containing aryl sulfonamides over 6-9 months. Resistant pools and clones will be isolated and characterized via whole-exome sequencing and transcriptomic analysis to identify mutations and expression changes. Resistance mechanisms will be validated by reintroducing candidate mutations into naive cells using CRISPR-Cas9 and by kinetic/crystallographic studies of mutant CAIX enzymes with the inhibitors.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comprehensive map of potential resistance pathways to CAIX-targeted therapy. It will provide critical pre-clinical data on vulnerabilities that can be exploited to overcome resistance, directly informing the design of more robust inhibitors or drug combinations. The project will also yield novel CAIX mutant structures, enriching the understanding of inhibitor-enzyme interactions.
proposals.novelty: While CAIX inhibitor development is mature, systematic in vitro evolution to probe resistance is a novel, forward-looking approach in this field. Combining high-throughput cellular screening with detailed structural biology on evolved mutant enzymes moves beyond standard inhibition assays to a dynamic, evolutionary perspective.

proposals.title: Structural Elucidation of Carbonic Anhydrase-Inhibitor Complexes in Native Cellular Environments Using Single-Particle Cryo-Electron Microscopy
proposals.motivation: Understanding the structural dynamics of human carbonic anhydrase (CA) isoforms, particularly tumor-associated CA IX/XII, in complex with inhibitors is crucial for designing selective anticancer agents. While X-ray crystallography provides high-resolution snapshots, it often fails to capture the conformational flexibility and native-state interactions of these enzymes within a cellular context. Cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) offers a powerful, pre-2026 alternative to visualize protein complexes in near-native states, but its application to small, soluble proteins like CAs remains a significant technical challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that single-particle cryo-EM, combined with advanced image processing, can resolve the three-dimensional structures of CA isoforms in complex with novel sulfonamide and coumarin-based inhibitors directly from cellular lysates or membrane preparations, revealing binding poses and conformational changes not observable by crystallography.
proposals.method: We will express and purify human CA II, IX, and XII, and complex them with novel inhibitors synthesized in our lab (e.g., biguanide-aryl sulfonamides, 4-substituted coumarins). Samples will be plunge-frozen on cryo-EM grids. Data collection will be performed on a 300 keV Titan Krios microscope equipped with a K3 direct electron detector. For the challenging small (~30 kDa) CA targets, we will employ a scaffold protein (e.g., antibody Fab fragment) to increase particle size and facilitate alignment. Image processing will utilize RELION-3.1 for 2D classification, 3D refinement, and Bayesian particle polishing to achieve sub-3 Å resolution.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust cryo-EM pipeline for studying small, soluble metabolic enzymes like CAs, moving beyond the traditional membrane protein focus of the technique. It will provide the first structural insights into inhibitor binding to CA isoforms within a near-native, solution-like environment, revealing dynamic features critical for drug design. The findings will directly inform the rational development of isoform-selective CA inhibitors with improved therapeutic profiles.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in adapting single-particle cryo-EM, a technique predominantly used for large complexes, to solve high-resolution structures of small, non-membrane-bound carbonic anhydrases in complex with inhibitors, bypassing the need for crystallization and capturing solution-state dynamics.

proposals.title: Targeting Carbonic Anhydrase IX Dimerization: Design and Synthesis of Sulfonamide-Based Protein-Protein Interaction Disruptors
proposals.motivation: Carbonic anhydrase IX (CA IX) is a validated anticancer target, but its role in tumor acidification and signaling is not solely dependent on its catalytic activity; its homodimerization and interactions with other proteins (e.g., bicarbonate transporters) are crucial for its function and localization. Pre-2026, CA inhibitors primarily target the enzyme's active site, potentially missing key regulatory mechanisms. Developing compounds that disrupt CA IX protein-protein interactions (PPIs) could offer a novel therapeutic strategy with a distinct mechanism of action and potentially overcome limitations of classical inhibitors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sulfonamide derivatives, traditionally designed as zinc-binding active-site inhibitors, can be structurally modified to bind at the CA IX dimerization interface or other allosteric sites, thereby selectively disrupting its critical protein-protein interactions without directly inhibiting its enzymatic activity.
proposals.method: We will employ a structure-based drug design approach. First, molecular dynamics simulations (using GROMACS) of the CA IX homodimer and its complex with known partners will identify key interfacial 'hot spots'. Using this data, we will perform virtual screening (with AutoDock Vina and Glide) of an in-house library of sulfonamide derivatives, focusing on compounds predicted to bind these interfacial regions. Selected hits will be synthesized via established routes for sulfonamide and coumarin-sulfonamide hybrids, as per Supuran's expertise. Their biological evaluation will involve surface plasmon resonance (SPR) to measure PPI disruption, alongside standard enzymatic inhibition assays to confirm the decoupling of PPI disruption from catalytic inhibition.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for repurposing the sulfonamide pharmacophore from enzyme inhibition to PPI disruption within the CA field. It will deliver first-in-class chemical tools to probe CA IX biology beyond catalysis and validate a new target site for anticancer drug discovery. The project will expand the therapeutic potential of CA-targeted compounds.
proposals.novelty: While PPI disruptors are known in other fields, applying this strategy to carbonic anhydrases, specifically by re-engineering the classic sulfonamide zinc-binding group to target protein interfaces, is a fundamentally new direction. It inverts the primary target from the conserved catalytic pocket to a more isoform-specific protein surface.

proposals.title: Rational Design of Broad-Spectrum Sulfonamide Inhibitors Targeting Pathogenic Bacterial and Fungal Carbonic Anhydrases for Anti-Infective Applications
proposals.motivation: Current anti-infective strategies often target single pathogens, risking treatment failure in polymicrobial infections or rapid resistance development. Pre-2026 research by Supuran's group and others has established the essential role of carbonic anhydrases (CAs) in the virulence and survival of diverse pathogens, including Escherichia coli and fungi. A single agent inhibiting CAs across multiple pathogen families could offer a powerful new therapeutic paradigm, addressing complex infections and potentially slowing resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by strategically modifying the zinc-binding sulfonamide pharmacophore and its flanking moieties, we can synthesize novel compounds that potently inhibit evolutionarily conserved CA active sites from distinct pathogenic bacteria and fungi, while maintaining selectivity over critical human CA isoforms.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of sulfonamides featuring variable aromatic/heterocyclic tails and linkers, informed by pre-2026 crystallographic data of pathogen CAs. Inhibition constants (Ki) will be determined for recombinant CAs from model bacterial (e.g., E. coli CAs, Helicobacter pylori CA) and fungal (e.g., Candida albicans Nce103) pathogens, alongside human off-target isoforms (hCA I, II, IX, XII). Lead compounds will be evaluated for in vitro antimicrobial efficacy in growth inhibition assays against corresponding pathogens. Molecular docking (using AutoDock Vina or GOLD) and molecular dynamics simulations will guide structure-activity relationship analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first rationally designed, broad-spectrum anti-infective CA inhibitors, expanding the therapeutic scope of sulfonamides beyond traditional selectivity. It will provide a detailed pharmacophore model for multi-pathogen CA inhibition and identify promising lead candidates for further development against polymicrobial and resistant infections.
proposals.novelty: While Supuran's pre-2026 work extensively explored selective inhibitors for single human or pathogen CA isoforms, this proposal deliberately inverts the selectivity paradigm to purposefully engineer broad-spectrum activity across pathogen kingdoms, a conceptually distinct and underexplored strategy in CA inhibitor design.

proposals.title: Design, Synthesis, and Evaluation of Novel Sugar-Sulfamate Conjugates as Antiviral Agents Targeting Viral Carbonic Anhydrases
proposals.motivation: Viral infections, particularly from herpesviruses and betacoronaviruses, remain a significant global health burden. Pre-2026 research has identified viral carbonic anhydrases (CAs) as promising antiviral targets, as they are crucial for viral replication and are absent in humans. Sulfamate-based inhibitors are potent CA inhibitors, but their application as antivirals is limited by selectivity and pharmacokinetic properties.
proposals.hypothesis: Systematic structural modification of the sugar scaffold in sulfamate conjugates will optimize binding affinity and selectivity for viral CAs over human isoforms, thereby enhancing antiviral potency and reducing off-target effects.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of sugar-sulfamate conjugates by varying the sugar core (e.g., glucose, galactose, mannose derivatives) and the sulfamate linker chemistry. All compounds will be screened for inhibition against recombinant human CA I, II, IX, XII and viral CAs (e.g., VCA from HCMV, nsp10 from SARS-CoV-2) using stopped-flow CO2 hydratase assays. The most selective inhibitors will be evaluated for antiviral activity in cell-based models of viral infection (e.g., HCMV, MERS-CoV). Lead compounds will be co-crystallized with target CAs for X-ray structure analysis to guide further optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish structure-activity relationships (SAR) linking specific sugar modifications to antiviral potency and CA isoform selectivity. It will deliver novel, optimized lead compounds targeting viral CAs and provide high-resolution structural data on inhibitor-viral CA binding, advancing the field of antiviral CA inhibitors.
proposals.novelty: While sulfamate CA inhibitors are well-known, their systematic conjugation to diverse, pharmacologically privileged sugar scaffolds for the explicit purpose of enhancing antiviral selectivity represents a novel and unexplored chemical strategy within pre-2026 knowledge.

proposals.title: Computational Prediction and Validation of Novel Viral and Cancer-Associated Carbonic Anhydrase Homologs as Targets for Sulfonamide Inhibitors
proposals.motivation: The carbonic anhydrase (CA) enzyme family, particularly isoforms like CA IX/XII, are validated anti-cancer targets, and bacterial CAs are emerging antimicrobial targets. However, the potential of CA inhibitors (CAIs) against viral or other cancer-associated proteins remains underexplored due to a lack of structural data for putative, non-human CA homologs. Pre-2026, AlphaFold2 has revolutionized protein structure prediction but has not been systematically applied to discover novel CA-like viral or cancer targets for the sulfonamide chemotype.
