proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that AlphaFold2 can accurately model the 3D structures of putative, uncharacterized carbonic anhydrase homologs from viral and cancer genomes, and that molecular docking will identify sulfonamide-based compounds with high predicted binding affinity, revealing new therapeutic targets.
proposals.method: We will first use sequence homology searches (BLAST, HMMER) against viral and cancer genome databases to identify putative CA-like proteins with low sequence identity to human isoforms. Their full-length structures will be predicted using AlphaFold2 (v2.3.1) with default parameters and multiple sequence alignments. High-confidence models (pLDDT > 80) will be prepared for docking using AutoDock Vina or Glide. A focused library of 200-300 synthesized sulfonamides and coumarins from the Supuran group's repository will be docked, prioritizing compounds with known selectivity profiles. Top-ranked virtual hits will be selected for synthesis and in vitro enzymatic inhibition assays against the identified targets.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first structural models of novel viral/cancer-associated CA homologs and computationally identify lead sulfonamide inhibitors, expanding the therapeutic landscape of CAIs. It will provide a validated pipeline for target discovery and rational design of inhibitors against previously unexplored CA-related proteins.
proposals.novelty: While AlphaFold2 is used for single-protein prediction, its systematic application to mine genomes for novel CA-like drug targets and directly couple this with a bespoke sulfonamide library for virtual screening is a novel, target-agnostic discovery strategy not yet reported in the CAI field.

proposals.title: Metabolic Flux Analysis of Cancer Cells to Elucidate the Antimetabolic Mode of Action of Isoform-Selective Sulfonamide Carbonic Anhydrase Inhibitors
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that tumor-associated carbonic anhydrase isoforms (CA IX/XII) are crucial for maintaining intracellular pH and fueling cancer cell metabolism, making them prime therapeutic targets. While many potent sulfonamide inhibitors have been synthesized, their precise metabolic consequences beyond enzyme inhibition remain poorly characterized. A systematic, quantitative analysis of how these inhibitors rewire central carbon metabolism is needed to understand their full anticancer potential and identify metabolic vulnerabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that selective inhibition of CA IX/XII by novel sulfonamide compounds induces specific, quantifiable disruptions in glycolytic and mitochondrial metabolic fluxes, and that these flux alterations correlate with and predict their antiproliferative efficacy in cancer cells.
proposals.method: We will apply 13C-glucose and 13C-glutamine metabolic flux analysis (MFA), a technique well-established in microbiology and systems biology, to human cancer cell lines (e.g., HeLa, MDA-MB-231) treated with a panel of pre-2026 synthesized sulfonamides, including 4-substituted coumarins and biguanide-containing aryl sulfonamides. Cells will be cultured with labeled substrates, and intracellular metabolite labeling patterns will be quantified via LC-MS. Flux distributions will be calculated using established computational platforms like INCA or OpenFLUX, which employ isotopomer network compartmental analysis and least-squares regression to infer in vivo reaction rates. This will be correlated with cell viability, extracellular acidification rate (ECAR), and oxygen consumption rate (OCR).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative fluxomic profile of sulfonamide CA inhibitors, moving beyond IC50 values to a functional systems-level understanding of their metabolic impact. It will identify which metabolic pathways (e.g., glycolysis, TCA cycle, pentose phosphate pathway) are most sensitive to CA inhibition, offering new biomarkers for drug efficacy. The data will also inform the rational design of next-generation inhibitors that optimally disrupt cancer cell metabolism.
proposals.novelty: While MFA is a standard tool in microbial physiology, its application to dissect the mechanism of carbonic anhydrase inhibitors in cancer cells is novel. This approach uniquely quantifies the direct metabolic consequences of isoform-selective inhibition, a dimension missing from standard enzymatic and cytotoxicity assays in the field.

proposals.title: Antibody-Drug Conjugates Targeting Carbonic Anhydrase IX with Novel Sulfonamide Warheads for Hypoxic Solid Tumors
proposals.motivation: Carbonic anhydrase IX (CA IX) is a well-validated, tumor-specific antigen overexpressed in hypoxic solid tumors and critical for pH regulation and metastasis. While potent sulfonamide inhibitors of CA IX exist, their systemic administration lacks tumor selectivity, limiting therapeutic efficacy and increasing off-target effects. Antibody-drug conjugate (ADC) technology, proven clinically with agents like trastuzumab emtansine, offers a powerful strategy to deliver cytotoxic payloads precisely, but has not been applied to deliver CA-inhibiting warheads.
proposals.hypothesis: Conjugating novel, high-affinity sulfonamide-based CA IX inhibitors to a monoclonal antibody targeting CA IX will generate an ADC that selectively delivers the inhibitor to tumor cells, resulting in superior antitumor activity and reduced systemic toxicity compared to the free inhibitor.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a panel of 4-substituted coumarin-sulfonamides and biguanide-aryl sulfonamides, previously shown to be potent and selective CA IX/XII inhibitors, with chemical handles (e.g., PEG linkers) for antibody conjugation. These will be conjugated via cleavable (e.g., Val-Cit) and non-cleavable linkers to the humanized anti-CA IX antibody M75. The ADCs will be characterized (DAR, stability) and evaluated for CA inhibition (stopped-flow CO2 hydrase assay), selective binding (flow cytometry), and internalization in CA IX-expressing HT-29 cells. In vivo efficacy and toxicity will be assessed in HT-29 xenograft models in nude mice.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first proof-of-concept for a CA IX-targeted ADC using a non-cytotoxic, enzyme-inhibiting warhead, expanding the ADC paradigm beyond traditional microtubule or DNA-damaging agents. It will deliver a novel therapeutic candidate for hypoxic tumors and provide a generalizable platform for the targeted delivery of carbonic anhydrase inhibitors.
proposals.novelty: While Supuran's group has developed nanoparticle delivery for CA inhibitors and potent selective inhibitors, the direct fusion of these selective sulfonamide warheads with antibody-targeting via ADC technology is a novel and unexplored therapeutic strategy. This approach moves beyond simple inhibition to targeted, internalized delivery of CA IX inhibitors.

proposals.title: Design and Synthesis of Nanomolar-Affinity, Fluorophore-Conjugated Sulfonamides as Single-Molecule Probes for Carbonic Anhydrase Isoforms in Live Cells
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the precise subcellular localization and real-time dynamics of specific carbonic anhydrase (CA) isoforms, particularly tumor-associated CA IX and XII, remain poorly visualized due to a lack of ultra-high-affinity, isoform-selective fluorescent probes. Existing sulfonamide inhibitors often lack the sub-nanomolar affinity required for stable single-molecule imaging, limiting insights into enzyme function in complex cellular environments. This gap hinders the understanding of CA roles in pathologies like cancer and their response to targeted inhibitors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by systematically engineering the sulfonamide scaffold with extended, rigid aromatic linkers and specific fluorophores, we can develop probes with dissociation constants (Kd) in the low nanomolar to picomolar range, enabling stable, specific binding for single-molecule fluorescence imaging of target CA isoforms in live cells.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of sulfonamides based on high-affinity scaffolds (e.g., from coumarin or biguanide-aryl sulfonamide hybrids), incorporating rigid, conjugated linkers (e.g., phenylacetylene) to minimize entropic penalty upon binding. These will be conjugated to photostable fluorophores (e.g., Cy5, ATTO 647N) suitable for single-molecule localization microscopy (SMLM). Affinity will be determined via stopped-flow kinetic assays (KI) and isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC). Selectivity will be profiled against recombinant human CA I, II, IX, and XII. Probe utility will be validated in live cancer cells using SMLM (dSTORM/PALM) to visualize CA IX/XII membrane dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first suite of sulfonamide-based chemical tools with the requisite affinity and photophysical properties for single-molecule tracking of CA isoforms. It will provide unprecedented spatial and temporal resolution of CA localization and mobility in live cells, directly informing drug targeting strategies. The developed design principles will be broadly applicable for creating high-affinity probes for other enzyme targets.
proposals.novelty: While many CA inhibitors exist, the deliberate design for single-molecule imaging—requiring a combination of extreme affinity (Kd < 1 nM), isoform selectivity, and appropriate photophysics—is a novel, unmet challenge. This moves beyond bulk inhibition assays to direct, quantitative visualization of target engagement at the molecular level in a native cellular context.

proposals.title: Rational Design of Multi-Target Sulfonamide Inhibitors for the Concurrent Modulation of Carbonic Anhydrase IX/XII and Receptor Tyrosine Kinases in Cancer
proposals.motivation: Current targeted cancer therapies often fail due to tumor heterogeneity, adaptive resistance, and signaling network redundancy. While single-target carbonic anhydrase (CA) IX/XII inhibitors show promise in hypoxic tumors, their efficacy is limited. Pre-2026 research highlights the need for polypharmacology—designing single agents that hit multiple key nodes in a cancer network—to improve therapeutic outcomes and overcome resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a rationally designed sulfonamide-based scaffold can be engineered to potently and selectively inhibit tumor-associated CA IX and XII isoforms while simultaneously inhibiting one or more receptor tyrosine kinases (e.g., VEGFR2, EGFR) critical for cancer progression, thereby achieving superior anti-tumor and anti-metastatic effects.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of novel sulfonamides by conjugating known CA-inhibitory zinc-binding groups (e.g., from acetazolamide or coumarin-based derivatives) to privileged heterocyclic motifs known to target kinase ATP-binding pockets (e.g., quinazoline, pyrimidine). Compounds will be screened for inhibition against recombinant human CA I, II, IX, XII and a panel of kinases. The most promising multi-target hits will be optimized using structure-based design informed by X-ray crystallography (as used in Supuran's recent work) and molecular docking simulations. Biological evaluation will include cytotoxicity, anti-migratory assays, and studies on HIF-1α signaling in 3D spheroid models.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new class of dual/multi-target anticancer agents, providing a chemical blueprint for sulfonamide-based polypharmacology. It will advance the understanding of structure-activity relationships for concurrent CA and kinase inhibition and establish a proof-of-concept for targeting interconnected hypoxic and growth factor signaling pathways with a single molecule.
proposals.novelty: While multi-target kinase inhibitors exist, and CA IX/XII inhibitors are well-studied, the deliberate integration of these two distinct pharmacophores into a single sulfonamide scaffold to create a unified multi-network modulator is a novel and unexplored strategy in anticancer drug design.

proposals.title: Computational Design and Biochemical Validation of Sulfonamide-Resistant Human Carbonic Anhydrase II Mutants for Research and Potential Gene Therapy Applications
proposals.motivation: Sulfonamide-based carbonic anhydrase (CA) inhibitors are widely used in therapy (e.g., acetazolamide) and as research tools, but their non-selectivity can complicate mechanistic studies and limit their therapeutic window. Engineering CA isoforms that retain native catalytic activity while becoming insensitive to these common inhibitors would provide powerful orthogonal tools for dissecting CA function in complex biological systems and could form the basis for future gene therapy strategies where endogenous CA activity needs to be protected from drug-induced inhibition.
proposals.hypothesis: Targeted mutations in the active site zinc-binding region and the sulfonamide-binding pocket of human CA II (hCA II) can be rationally designed to abrogate sulfonamide binding while preserving, or only minimally affecting, the enzyme's catalytic efficiency for CO2 hydration.
proposals.method: We will employ a two-stage computational design pipeline. First, using pre-2026 structural data (e.g., PDB IDs: 3HS4, 4QYF), RosettaDesign and FoldX will be used for in silico saturation mutagenesis of residues within 8Å of the sulfonamide nitrogen coordinating the zinc ion (e.g., T199, E106, H94, H96, H119), selecting mutants predicted to disrupt inhibitor hydrogen-bonding networks and van der Waals contacts. Second, selected designs will be synthesized via site-directed mutagenesis, expressed in E. coli, and purified. Their catalytic activity (kcat/KM) and inhibition profiles (KI) against a panel of sulfonamides (acetazolamide, benzolamide, ethoxzolamide) will be determined using the stopped-flow CO2 hydration assay, with crystal structures of key mutants solved to validate design predictions.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first set of biochemically validated, sulfonamide-resistant hCA II mutants, providing a novel research tool for cell-based studies where specific CA activity must be isolated. It will also establish a proof-of-concept design framework for engineering drug-resistant CA isoforms, opening a new avenue in CA biology and potential gene therapy applications for CA-related disorders.
proposals.novelty: While extensive pre-2026 work by Supuran and others focuses on designing inhibitors for specific CA isoforms, the inverse problem—designing the enzyme target to be resistant to an entire class of well-established drugs—is a novel and underexplored strategy that flips the traditional drug discovery paradigm.

proposals.title: Design, Synthesis, and Biological Evaluation of Novel Carbonic Anhydrase Activators for the Modulation of Tumor pH and Sensitization to Chemotherapy
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that tumor hypoxia induces the overexpression of CA IX and XII, which acidify the extracellular microenvironment, promoting invasion, metastasis, and chemoresistance. The dominant therapeutic strategy has been selective inhibition of these isoforms. However, a paradoxical, unexplored approach is to activate specific intracellular CAs (e.g., CA II, III) to potentially enhance intracellular buffering capacity, counteract extracellular acidosis, and sensitize cancer cells to cytotoxic agents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted activation of specific cytosolic carbonic anhydrase isoforms in cancer cells will modulate intracellular pH dynamics, disrupt the tumor's adaptive acid-base balance, and increase susceptibility to conventional chemotherapy.
proposals.method: We will synthesize novel CA activators based on amino acid and histamine scaffolds, incorporating structural motifs from our coumarin and sulfonamide inhibitor libraries to ensure isoform selectivity. Activation kinetics (kcat/KM enhancement) will be determined for human CA I, II, III, IX, and XII using a stopped-flow CO2 hydration assay. The most promising activators will be evaluated in 2D and 3D cultures of hypoxic cancer cells for effects on intra- and extracellular pH (using fluorescent probes), cell viability, and synergy with agents like cisplatin and doxorubicin. X-ray crystallography of activator-CA complexes will guide structure-based optimization.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first structure-activity relationship (SAR) profile for selective CA activators in an oncological context. It will provide proof-of-concept that CA activation is a viable therapeutic strategy, potentially opening a new avenue for combination cancer therapy. The project will deliver novel chemical tools to probe CA-related pH physiology in tumors.
proposals.novelty: While CA inhibitors are extensively studied, the systematic design of CA activators for cancer therapy is unprecedented. This proposal inverts the core pharmacological strategy in the CA field from inhibition to targeted activation, challenging the prevailing paradigm.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Activity Profiling of Carbonic Anhydrase IX and XII in Tumour Spheroids Using a Microfluidic Droplet-Based Assay
proposals.motivation: The tumour-associated carbonic anhydrase (CA) isoforms IX and XII are critical for pH regulation, tumour progression, and metastasis, making them prime therapeutic targets. However, current bulk assays mask the significant heterogeneity in CA expression and function among individual cancer cells within a tumour, limiting our understanding of drug resistance and the precise targeting of hypoxic niches. Developing single-cell resolution tools is therefore essential for advancing personalized anti-CA therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within a 3D tumour spheroid model, there exists a heterogeneous distribution of catalytically active CA IX and XII at the single-cell level, which correlates with cellular hypoxia markers and resistance to conventional CA inhibitors.
proposals.method: We will synthesize membrane-impermeant, fluorescent activity-based probes (ABPs) selective for the extracellular active site of CA IX/XII, building on our coumarin and sulfonamide inhibitor scaffolds. Single cells from disaggregated tumour spheroids will be co-encapsulated with these ABPs and a viability dye in picoliter droplets using a microfluidic device (e.g., Drop-seq platform). The enzymatic conversion of the probe will generate a fluorescent signal quantifiable via high-throughput flow cytometry of broken droplets. Parallel single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) from the same droplets will link CA activity to isoform-specific transcript levels.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first integrated platform to directly quantify the catalytic activity of specific CA isoforms in single cells alongside their transcriptional profile. It will provide an unprecedented map of functional CA heterogeneity in tumours, identifying subpopulations with high CA activity that may drive acidosis and evasion. This will enable the rational design of inhibitor-nanoparticle conjugates, like our hyaluronic acid systems, to target these resistant cells.
proposals.novelty: While scRNA-seq reveals CA isoform mRNA, this approach directly measures the *functional activity* of CA enzymes at the single-cell level, a crucial distinction given post-translational regulation. The combination of custom ABPs, droplet microfluidics, and linked phenotypic-transcriptomic readouts for CAs is novel and moves beyond static immunohistochemistry or bulk kinetic studies.

proposals.title: Therapeutic Rationale for Multi-Isoform Carbonic Anhydrase Inhibition: Synthesis and Evaluation of Broad-Spectrum Inhibitors in Complex Cancer Models
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 carbonic anhydrase (CA) drug design overwhelmingly prioritizes isoform-selective inhibitors, particularly targeting tumor-associated CA IX/XII, to minimize off-target effects. However, cancer is a complex, adaptive disease where multiple CA isoforms (e.g., cytosolic CA II, mitochondrial CA V) contribute to interconnected pathways of pH regulation, metabolism, and metastasis. The therapeutic potential of simultaneously modulating several key isoforms remains largely unexplored, representing a significant gap in the field.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that rationally designed, broad-spectrum CA inhibitors, which simultaneously inhibit a defined panel of tumor-relevant isoforms (CA II, IX, XII, and V), will demonstrate superior anti-tumor and anti-metastatic efficacy compared to highly selective CA IX/XII inhibitors in complex in vitro and in vivo cancer models.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of coumarin and sulfonamide derivatives, building on our group's expertise, designed with chemical features predicted (via molecular docking using AutoDock Vina) to bind conserved active-site residues across multiple human CA isoforms. Inhibitory activity (KI) will be kinetically assessed against recombinant human CA I, II, IV, IX, XII, and V. The most promising multi-isoform inhibitors will be evaluated in 3D spheroid co-culture models of renal and glioblastoma cancer cells for effects on extracellular acidification (via SNARF-1 staining), cell invasion, and viability. Lead compounds will progress to efficacy studies in relevant murine xenograft models, assessing tumor growth and metastasis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a systematic, proof-of-concept evaluation of multi-isoform CA inhibition as a viable therapeutic strategy, challenging the dominant selectivity paradigm. It will deliver novel chemical tools and pre-clinical data establishing a potential correlation between a defined inhibitory profile and efficacy against complex tumor phenotypes, such as invasion and metabolic adaptation.
proposals.novelty: While others seek maximal selectivity, this proposal deliberately explores the therapeutic 'sweet spot' of controlled polypharmacology within the CA enzyme family. It moves beyond targeting single isoforms to strategically disrupt the pH-regulatory network that cancers exploit, a concept not rigorously tested with purpose-designed compounds pre-2026.

proposals.title: Design, Synthesis, and Biological Evaluation of Novel Carbonic Anhydrase Activators Targeting Isoforms II and VII for the Potential Treatment of Cognitive Deficits
proposals.motivation: While extensive pre-2026 research by Supuran and others has focused on CA inhibitors for cancer and glaucoma, the therapeutic potential of CA *activation* remains underexplored. Specific CA isoforms (e.g., CA II and VII) are crucial for neuronal signaling and pH balance in the brain, and their deficiency or reduced activity is implicated in cognitive disorders and certain forms of Alzheimer's disease. Developing isoform-specific activators offers a novel pharmacological strategy to correct these enzymatic deficits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that novel, drug-like molecules can be designed to selectively activate human CA II and VII isoforms, and that such activation will enhance enzymatic activity in cellular models relevant to neuronal function, providing a proof-of-concept for a new therapeutic approach.
proposals.method: We will employ a structure-based drug design approach, using X-ray crystallographic data (pre-2026) of CA II and VII to identify key residues in their activator-binding sites. A focused library of compounds, including coumarin and aryl sulfonamide derivatives modified with known activator scaffolds (e.g., histamine, L-adrenaline analogs), will be synthesized. Their CA activation profiles will be kinetically characterized against a panel of 12 human CA isoforms using a stopped-flow CO2 hydration assay. The most selective activators will be evaluated in neuronal cell cultures for their ability to modulate intracellular pH and rescue activity in models of CA deficiency.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new chemical library of selective CA activators, directly expanding the pharmacological toolbox beyond inhibitors. It will provide crucial structure-activity relationship (SAR) data for activation, a less-studied mechanism, and deliver in vitro proof-of-concept for targeting CA deficiency, opening a new research avenue in neuropharmacology.
proposals.novelty: This proposal inverts the dominant inhibitor-focused paradigm in the CA field to specifically design *activators* as therapeutics. It moves beyond the known, non-selective natural amine activators to create novel, synthetically tailored compounds with isoform selectivity, a concept not fully realized in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Computational Prediction of Novel Allosteric Sites in Human Carbonic Anhydrase Isoforms via Transfer Learning from Structurally Diverse Enzyme Families
proposals.motivation: Current carbonic anhydrase (CA) drug discovery focuses overwhelmingly on active-site inhibitors, which can lack isoform selectivity and lead to side effects. Allosteric modulation offers a promising route to achieve high selectivity and novel mechanisms of inhibition, but experimentally identified allosteric sites for CAs are scarce. A systematic computational approach to predict potential allosteric sites is urgently needed to guide the rational design of next-generation CA modulators.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that machine learning models trained on known allosteric sites from a diverse set of enzyme families can be effectively transferred to predict novel, druggable allosteric pockets on human CA isoforms, particularly the cancer-associated CA IX and XII.
proposals.method: We will employ the Allosite and AllositePro algorithms to perform initial predictions on key CA isoforms (CA I, II, IX, XII). To enhance accuracy, we will implement a transfer learning approach using a convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture pre-trained on the Allosteric Database (ASD) and fine-tuned with CA-specific structural features (e.g., dynamic residue networks from molecular dynamics simulations). Predictions will be validated in silico via molecular docking of fragment libraries and by comparing predicted sites with known mutational and structural data.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comprehensive map of predicted allosteric sites across pharmacologically relevant human CA isoforms. It will provide a ranked list of high-confidence, novel targets for synthetic chemists to design selective allosteric inhibitors or activators, directly enabling new therapeutic strategies for cancer, glaucoma, and other CA-mediated diseases.
proposals.novelty: While allosteric site prediction tools exist, their application to the CA family has been minimal and non-systematic. This proposal innovates by specifically applying and adapting transfer learning methodologies from broader enzyme families to the CA superfamily, creating a tailored predictive framework for this high-value target class.

proposals.title: Chemoproteomic Profiling of Novel Coumarin and Sulfonamide Inhibitors Reveals Isoform-Specific Engagement in Human Cancer Cells
proposals.motivation: Selective inhibition of cancer-associated carbonic anhydrase isoforms (CA IX/XII) is a validated therapeutic strategy, but achieving true isoform selectivity in the complex cellular milieu remains a major challenge. Current evaluations rely heavily on purified enzyme assays, which fail to capture the competitive binding landscape, subcellular localization, and protein-protein interactions present in living cells, leading to discrepancies between in vitro potency and cellular activity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying activity-based protein profiling (ABPP) with photoreactive probes derived from novel coumarin and sulfonamide chemotypes will enable the direct, quantitative mapping of inhibitor engagement across all active CA isoforms in cancer cells, revealing true isoform selectivity profiles and off-target interactions not apparent in traditional assays.
proposals.method: We will synthesize photoreactive activity-based probes (ABPs) by conjugating a benzophenone crosslinker and an alkyne handle to our most promising 4-substituted coumarin and biguanide-containing aryl sulfonamide inhibitors. Following treatment of live CA-expressing HT-29 cancer cells with these ABPs and UV-induced crosslinking, engaged proteins will be conjugated to biotin-azide via click chemistry, enriched on streptavidin beads, and identified by liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS). Quantitative proteomics using tandem mass tag (TMT) labeling will compare engagement profiles across inhibitor concentrations and isoforms.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first chemoproteomic map for coumarin-based CA inhibitors in cells, directly linking chemical structure to isoform engagement. It will provide a critical dataset for validating the selectivity claims of next-generation CA inhibitors and identify potential off-targets that could influence drug efficacy or toxicity, thereby bridging the gap between enzymatic inhibition and cellular pharmacology.
proposals.novelty: While ABPP is established in enzymology, its application to the CA field has been minimal, and never before applied to profile the novel 4-substituted coumarin class developed by our group. This approach moves beyond kinetic inhibition constants (Ki) to measure direct, competitive target occupancy within the native cellular proteome.

proposals.title: Targeted Pathogen Disruption: Design and Synthesis of Dual Inhibitors for the Complete Carbonic Anhydrase Repertoire of Helicobacter pylori
proposals.motivation: Pathogenic bacteria and parasites often encode multiple carbonic anhydrase (CA) isoforms crucial for their survival, virulence, and pH homeostasis. Current CA inhibitor development, exemplified by work on human CA IX/XII, often focuses on single isoforms, which may lead to incomplete therapeutic efficacy and resistance. A strategy to simultaneously inhibit the organism's entire CA set could offer a more potent and evolutionarily robust antimicrobial approach.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by computationally analyzing the structural differences within the complete CA set of a specific pathogen, we can design and synthesize multi-targeted inhibitors that potently and selectively inhibit all its CA isoforms, leading to superior antimicrobial effects compared to single-isoform inhibition.
proposals.method: We will select Helicobacter pylori (encoding α-CA and β-CA isoforms) as the model pathogen. Using pre-2026 structural data (PDB) for H. pylori CAs and human off-targets (CA I, II), we will perform comparative molecular dynamics simulations and binding pocket analysis. A focused library of benzenesulfonamide derivatives, incorporating variable tail groups and linkers, will be designed in silico. Compounds will be prioritized using ensemble docking (AutoDock Vina, Glide) against all target CAs simultaneously, scoring for balanced affinity. Top candidates will be synthesized, and their inhibitory profiles (KIs) against recombinant H. pylori CAs and key human isoforms will be determined via stopped-flow CO2 hydration assay. Antimicrobial activity will be validated in vitro.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first rationally designed, dual-target inhibitors for the complete CA set of a major human pathogen, establishing a novel 'pan-pathogen-CA' inhibition paradigm. It will provide a chemical toolbox and a validated computational workflow that can be applied to other infectious agents, advancing the field of anti-infective CA inhibitors beyond single-target approaches.
proposals.novelty: While selective inhibition of human CA isoforms is well-established, the deliberate design of a single compound series to potently inhibit all CA isoforms from a specific pathogen is a novel, organism-centric strategy. This shifts the focus from 'isoform-selective' to 'pathogen-selective' inhibition.

proposals.title: Design and Synthesis of Polypharmacological Carbonic Anhydrase Inhibitors to Systemically Modulate Isoform Networks in Hypoxic Cancer Cells
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including from our group, has focused on developing isoform-selective inhibitors (e.g., for CA IX/XII) to target pathological processes like tumor hypoxia. However, in vivo, multiple CA isoforms (e.g., CA II, IX, XII) are co-expressed and functionally interconnected, forming a compensatory network. Targeting a single isoform may lead to limited efficacy due to network resilience, highlighting the need for a systems-level pharmacological intervention.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single chemical entity, designed to simultaneously and differentially inhibit a defined subset of co-expressed CA isoforms (e.g., CA IX, XII, and II), will more effectively disrupt the tumor pH regulatory network and induce synergistic anti-proliferative effects compared to a highly selective single-isoform inhibitor.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a library of dual/triple-target inhibitors based on a coumarin or biguanide-sulfonamide hybrid scaffold, known for isoform selectivity. Inhibitory activity (Ki) against recombinant human CA I, II, IX, and XII will be determined via stopped-flow CO2 hydratase assay. The most promising polypharmacological agents will be evaluated in 3D spheroid models of hypoxic cancer cells (e.g., HT-29). Network perturbation will be assessed by measuring intra- and extracellular pH dynamics (using fluorescent probes), lactate excretion, and transcriptomic changes of CA isoforms via qPCR.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first proof-of-concept for a 'network pharmacology' approach in the CA field, moving beyond single-target inhibition. It will deliver novel chemical tools to probe CA isoform crosstalk and establish a functional screening paradigm to evaluate systemic network modulation versus isolated enzyme inhibition.
proposals.novelty: While multi-target CA inhibitors exist, they are typically non-selective. This proposal deliberately engineers polypharmacology to rewire a specific, disease-relevant subset of the CA network, a strategy distinct from both pan-inhibition and ultra-selective inhibition.

proposals.title: Evaluation of the Sulfamate Carbonic Anhydrase Inhibitor S4 in Combination with Cisplatin for the Treatment of Cisplatin-Resistant Ovarian and Head and Neck Cancers
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established that tumor-associated carbonic anhydrase isoforms (CA IX/XII) contribute to chemotherapy resistance by regulating tumor pH, promoting survival, and inhibiting apoptosis. Cisplatin remains a cornerstone therapy for many solid tumors, but resistance is a major clinical hurdle. The sulfamate CA inhibitor S4 has shown promising synergistic effects with cisplatin in small cell lung cancer models, but its potential to overcome cisplatin resistance in other aggressive, CA IX/XII-expressing cancers remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the sulfamate carbonic anhydrase inhibitor S4 will sensitize cisplatin-resistant ovarian and head and neck cancer cells to cisplatin by disrupting pH regulation and enhancing apoptosis, and that this combination will show superior efficacy in vivo compared to either agent alone.
proposals.method: We will synthesize S4 using established sulfamate chemistry. In vitro, we will test the combination on a panel of cisplatin-sensitive and -resistant ovarian (e.g., A2780, SKOV-3) and head/neck (e.g., SCC-25, CAL-27) cancer cell lines, assessing viability (MTT assay), apoptosis (Annexin V/PI flow cytometry), and CA IX/XII activity (Wilbur-Anderson assay). Isobologram analysis will determine synergy. For in vivo validation, we will use established subcutaneous xenograft models of cisplatin-resistant disease in nude mice, treating with vehicle, cisplatin, S4, or the combination, and monitoring tumor growth, histology, and CA IX expression via immunohistochemistry.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide critical pre-clinical evidence for repurposing the CA inhibitor S4 as a cisplatin chemosensitizer in two new, clinically relevant cancer types. It will elucidate the mechanistic role of CA inhibition in overcoming cisplatin resistance beyond pH modulation, potentially involving pathways like apoptosis and metastasis. The data will form the foundation for future translational studies and combination therapy trials.
proposals.novelty: While S4-cisplatin synergy has been shown in lung cancer, its application to overcome intrinsic and acquired cisplatin resistance in ovarian and head/neck cancers, which strongly express the target isoforms CA IX/XII, is novel. The proposal uniquely focuses on translating a known synergistic pairing into new, resistant tumor contexts where the therapeutic need is high.

proposals.title: Design, Synthesis, and Biological Evaluation of Novel Carbonic Anhydrase Activators Targeting CA I and CA II for the Potential Treatment of Cognitive Deficits
proposals.motivation: While extensive pre-2026 research by Supuran and others has focused on CA inhibitors for cancer and glaucoma, the therapeutic potential of CA activators (CAAs) remains underexplored. Specific CA isoforms (e.g., cytosolic CA I and II) are crucial for neuronal pH regulation and CO2 hydration in the brain, with their deficiency implicated in cognitive disorders like Alzheimer's disease. Developing isoform-selective activators could offer a novel pharmacological strategy for enhancing synaptic efficiency and cognitive function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that novel, structurally distinct small molecules can be designed to selectively activate human CA I and/or CA II isoforms, and that such activation will enhance enzymatic activity in cellular models relevant to neuronal function, providing a proof-of-concept for treating diseases characterized by CA hypofunction.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a focused library of compounds based on known CAA scaffolds (e.g., histamine derivatives, amino acids) and novel coumarin- and aryl sulfonamide-based structures, informed by Supuran's expertise in CA inhibitor design. Compounds will be screened for activation of recombinant human CA I, II, VII, and XIII using a stopped-flow CO2 hydration assay. Active hits will be evaluated for isoform selectivity and cytotoxicity. The most promising activators will be co-crystallized with CA II for X-ray structural analysis to elucidate the activation mechanism. Their effect on intracellular pH and neuronal activity will be tested in primary astrocyte cultures.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first rationally designed library of CAAs with demonstrated selectivity for cytosolic brain-associated isoforms. It will provide critical structure-activity relationship (SAR) data for CA activation, a field dominated by inhibitor studies, and establish a robust in vitro pipeline for evaluating CAAs in neuronal models, opening a new therapeutic avenue.
proposals.novelty: This proposal directly inverts the dominant inhibitor-focused paradigm in the CA field by applying Supuran's established medicinal chemistry and crystallography expertise to the design of activators, targeting non-cancer indications (cognitive deficits) where CA enhancement, not inhibition, is the goal.

proposals.title: Investigating Metabolic Cross-Talk: The Interplay Between Glycolytic Lactate Production and Carbonic Anhydrase IX-Mediated CO2 Hydration in Hypoxic Tumor Cells
proposals.motivation: Solid tumors exhibit a hypoxic microenvironment that drives both upregulated glycolysis (Warburg effect) and the overexpression of carbonic anhydrase IX (CA IX). CA IX hydrates CO2 to produce protons and bicarbonate, contributing to extracellular acidification and tumor progression. While both pathways are independently linked to acidosis, the potential metabolic cross-talk and regulatory feedback between lactate production and CA IX activity remains poorly understood, limiting our ability to design synergistic therapeutic strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the glycolytic lactate production pathway and the CA IX-mediated CO2 hydration pathway operate independently under hypoxic stress, but their end-products (lactate/H+ and HCO3-/H+) create a synergistic acidic microenvironment that reciprocally regulates the activity and expression of key enzymes in both pathways.
proposals.method: We will synthesize novel dual-targeting compounds combining a sulfonamide-based CA IX inhibitor pharmacophore (informed by our recent crystallographic and coumarin studies) with a lactate dehydrogenase (LDH)-A inhibitor moiety. These compounds will be tested in hypoxic human cancer cell lines (e.g., HT-29, MDA-MB-231) using selective CA IX inhibitors (e.g., SLC-0111 analogues) and LDH-A inhibitors (e.g., FX-11) as controls. Intracellular and extracellular pH, lactate levels, and CA IX activity will be measured in real-time using fluorescent probes (BCECF, HPTS) and enzymatic assays. Gene and protein expression of CA IX, LDH-A, and HIF-1α will be analyzed via qPCR and western blot under combinatorial inhibition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct experimental evidence defining the relationship between two major acidifying pathways in tumors. It will elucidate whether combined pharmacological inhibition of CA IX and glycolysis/LDH-A offers a synergistic anti-tumor effect. The project will also deliver a new class of bifunctional chemical probes to dissect this metabolic interplay.
proposals.novelty: While CA IX and glycolysis are well-studied in isolation, systematically probing their functional interaction and testing combined inhibition with rationally designed hybrid molecules is a novel approach. This moves beyond characterizing single pathways to understanding integrated tumor metabolism.

proposals.title: Targeting Allosteric Sites and Protein-Protein Interfaces of Human Carbonic Anhydrase Isoforms: A Strategy for Enhanced Selectivity and Novel Therapeutic Modalities
proposals.motivation: The dominant paradigm in carbonic anhydrase (CA) drug discovery, championed by Prof. Supuran's group, focuses on designing high-affinity active-site inhibitors, primarily sulfonamides. While successful, this approach can face challenges in achieving absolute isoform selectivity and may be limited by off-target effects. Pre-2026 research increasingly recognizes that CA isoforms function within complex interactomes, suggesting that disrupting protein-protein interactions (PPIs) or targeting allosteric sites could offer superior selectivity and novel mechanisms of action, particularly for cancer-related isoforms like CA IX and XII.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, non-active site pockets exist on human CA isoforms, particularly the tumor-associated CA IX and XII, which can be targeted by small molecules to allosterically inhibit enzymatic activity or disrupt critical protein-protein interactions, thereby providing a new class of CA modulators with distinct pharmacological profiles.
proposals.method: We will employ an integrated structure-based and biophysical screening approach. First, molecular dynamics simulations (using GROMACS) and computational solvent mapping (FTMap) will be used to predict transient allosteric sites and PPI interfaces on CA IX/XII structures. A focused library of non-sulfonamide compounds, including coumarin and biguanide derivatives inspired by recent group work, will be synthesized and screened via surface plasmon resonance (SPR) to identify binders to recombinant CA isoforms. Hits will be characterized kinetically to confirm non-competitive inhibition and co-crystallized for X-ray structure determination to validate binding modes. Functional disruption of CA IX-PDGF-BB interaction will be assessed via ELISA.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish proof-of-concept for allosteric/PPI disruption as a viable strategy in CA inhibition, potentially yielding first-in-class chemical probes. It will expand the CA pharmacological toolbox beyond active-site zinc binders, providing new leads for cancer therapeutics with potentially improved selectivity. The project will also deliver high-resolution structural data on novel binding sites, guiding future drug design.
proposals.novelty: This proposal directly challenges the necessity of ultra-high affinity active-site binding, a cornerstone of the field, by deliberately pursuing lower-affinity, non-competitive modulators. It shifts the target from the conserved catalytic zinc to isoform-specific surface features, a fundamentally different approach not extensively explored in the CA literature pre-2026.

proposals.title: Design, Synthesis, and Kinetic Characterization of Bifunctional Ligands for the Selective Inhibition of Carbonic Anhydrase IX and Simultaneous Activation of Carbonic Anhydrase II
proposals.motivation: The development of isoform-selective carbonic anhydrase (CA) inhibitors is a major goal in medicinal chemistry for targeting diseases like cancer (CA IX/XII) and glaucoma. However, many pathological conditions involve dysregulation of multiple CA isoforms, where inhibiting one while compensating for the loss of function in another could offer superior therapeutic outcomes. A bifunctional molecule capable of this dual action represents an unexplored and potentially powerful pharmacological strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by strategically linking a zinc-binding sulfonamide moiety (for CA IX inhibition) to a tail containing an activator scaffold (e.g., histamine or amino acid derivatives known to bind the activator site of CA II), we can create single molecules that simultaneously and selectively inhibit tumor-associated CA IX while activating cytosolic CA II.
proposals.method: We will design candidate molecules using molecular docking (AutoDock Vina, GOLD) and molecular dynamics simulations (AMBER) to model interactions with the active sites of CA II (PDB: 3KS3) and CA IX-mimic (CA II with a mimic of the CA IX active site). Core synthetic strategies will involve coupling modified 4-substituted coumarin or biguanide-containing aryl sulfonamide scaffolds (known for CA IX inhibition) to known CA activator motifs via flexible linkers. The synthesized compounds will be evaluated for inhibition against recombinant human CA I, II, IX, and XII using the stopped-flow CO2 hydration assay, and for activation against CA II and VII.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first proof-of-concept for a single-molecule CA isoform regulator with opposing activities. It will provide a novel chemical toolkit for probing complex CA physiology and open a new avenue in drug design for conditions like cancers with hypoxic acidosis, where inhibiting CA IX and bolstering CA II activity could normalize pH dysregulation more effectively.
proposals.novelty: While CA inhibitors and activators are well-studied separately, the design of a unified pharmacophore possessing both activities for different isoforms is unprecedented. This moves beyond simple selectivity to achieve targeted functional modulation of the CA network.

ideas.title: Adapt a 'Theranostic' Trial Design from Nuclear Medicine for CAIX Inhibitors
ideas.description: This idea proposes adapting a solution from nuclear medicine—the 'theranostic' trial design—to the clinical translation of CAIX inhibitors for breast cancer. In the analogous field, radiopharmaceutical therapies for prostate cancer first use a diagnostic PET tracer to select patients whose tumors express high levels of the target (PSMA), thereby enriching the trial population for likely responders. The adapted research idea is to implement a similar two-step strategy: first, validate and employ a CAIX-targeted PET imaging agent (e.g., a CAIX-binding antibody fragment or small molecule conjugate) in a screening phase to identify breast cancer patients with tumors exhibiting high CAIX expression. These preselected patients would then be enrolled in clinical trials testing the novel CAIX-specific small molecule inhibitors, significantly increasing trial efficiency and the likelihood of demonstrating clinical efficacy by ensuring the therapeutic target is abundantly present.

ideas.title: Sequential CAIX Inhibition and Therapy Based on Pathogen Eradication Models
ideas.description: This idea adapts a sequential treatment model from antimicrobial therapy, where disrupting a protective microenvironment precedes a lethal attack. The analogous problem is eradicating persistent bacterial biofilms, solved by using agents to first disrupt the biofilm structure or stress adaptations, then applying antibiotics. Adapted here, the research would investigate a defined sequential protocol: a CAIX inhibitor is administered first to normalize the acidic tumor microenvironment and compromise cancer cell adaptations to hypoxia. This sensitization phase is then followed by standard chemotherapy or radiation, with the hypothesis that this temporal sequence will maximize the disruption of pro-survival and metastatic pathways, leading to significantly enhanced anti-tumor and anti-metastatic effects compared to concurrent combination or monotherapies.

ideas.description: This research direction integrates novel CAIX inhibitors into clinical trial design alongside the deployment of engineered FRET-based biosensors specific for DAG and sphingolipids. The biosensors are used in live-cell imaging of patient-derived organoids or xenografts from trial participants to monitor real-time changes in TGN lipid dynamics upon CAIX inhibition. This allows for the direct correlation of drug-induced metabolic stress, visualized as specific lipid signaling flares, with therapeutic response and tumor cell death. Consequently, the approach transforms the clinical trial by providing a mechanistic, real-time pharmacodynamic readout to stratify patients, optimize dosing, and identify early biomarkers of treatment efficacy or emerging resistance.

ideas.title: Develop a CAIX-targeted activatable 'smart' probe for hypoxia imaging
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from neuroscience molecular imaging, where activatable 'smart' probes are used for high-contrast detection of specific targets like beta-amyloid plaques. The analogous solution involves probes that are optically silent in circulation and become fluorescent only upon target-specific enzymatic activation. Adapted to the original research context, the idea is to develop a CAIX-targeted activatable imaging probe for tumor hypoxia. The probe would be designed with a fluorescence quencher linked via a substrate cleavable by a protease co-expressed in hypoxic tumors (e.g., MMP-2/9). Upon intravenous administration, the intact probe circulates with minimal background signal. When it binds to CAIX on hypoxic tumor cells and encounters the specific protease in the tumor microenvironment, the linker is cleaved, releasing the quencher and activating a strong, localized fluorescent signal. This activatable mechanism would provide a superior signal-to-noise ratio compared to always-on probes, enabling more precise non-invasive detection of tumor hypoxia and potentially more sensitive monitoring of treatment-induced changes in the tumor microenvironment.

ideas.title: Reconstituting CAIX-mediated stromal signaling in minimal synthetic membranes
ideas.description: This project fuses the stromal CAIX biology question with a synthetic membrane reconstitution approach. The core idea is to build minimal artificial liposomes that mimic the membrane composition of tumor-associated stromal cells, specifically incorporating CAIX and candidate lipid-sorting components from the Trans-Golgi Network (TGN). This defined system will be used to test if CAIX activity, through its regulation of pH and possibly its binding partners, directly drives the sorting of specific lipids (e.g., signaling lipids like lysophosphatidic acid or sphingosine-1-phosphate) into discrete membrane domains or synthetic EV buds. This will establish a direct, mechanistic link between stromal CAIX and the generation of pro-tumorigenic lipid signals, isolated from the complexity of the full tumor microenvironment.

ideas.title: TGN Lipid Dyshomeostasis as a Trigger for Caspase-Independent Cell Death via CAIX Depletion
ideas.description: This project investigates whether the caspase-independent cell death triggered by Carbonic Anhydrase IX (CAIX) depletion is executed through the disruption of conserved lipid functions at the Trans-Golgi Network (TGN). We hypothesize that CAIX loss, by perturbing intracellular pH and ion homeostasis, induces a specific lipid dysregulation at the TGN that is lethal across diverse eukaryotic cell types, including both specialized secretory cells and standard model lines. The research will compare TGN lipid composition, membrane dynamics, and downstream organelle stress signals following CAIX knockdown to define if a conserved lipid-centric pathway is the core executioner mechanism. This fusion directly connects a metabolic enzyme to organelle-specific lipid biology as a novel cell death trigger.

ideas.title: Imaging-Guided Spatiotemporal Targeting of Hypoxic Niches with CAIX Theranostics and Modulated Therapy
ideas.description: This adversarially improved idea proposes a closed-loop theranostic system to overcome tumor heterogeneity. It first employs non-invasive CAIX-specific PET imaging to dynamically map hypoxic, CAIX-expressing tumor sub-regions before and during treatment. This spatial map is then used to rationally guide therapy: modulating radiation dose painting to aggressively target resistant hypoxic niches and timing CAIX inhibitor pulses to coincide with imaging-confirmed target engagement. The approach rigorously tests whether adaptive, image-informed spatiotemporal targeting of CAIX improves therapeutic index and overcomes microenvironment-driven resistance compared to static combination regimens.

ideas.title: Adapting a 'Failsafe Circuit' Approach from Electrical Engineering to Map Caspase-Independent Cell Death
ideas.description: This idea uses an analogy from electrical engineering's failsafe circuit design to solve the problem of elucidating the caspase-independent cell death pathway triggered by CAIX depletion. In engineering, when a primary system fails, a pre-defined secondary circuit initiates a controlled shutdown; similarly, when the canonical caspase apoptosis pathway is compromised or not triggered, cells employ backup death mechanisms. The analogous solution—comprehensively mapping the system's network to identify critical components and activation triggers—is adapted by proposing a systematic, multi-parametric experimental approach. This involves using perturbations (CAIX depletion plus caspase inhibition) combined with high-throughput, dynamic measurements of cell death markers, organelle integrity, and metabolic states to construct a causal map of the molecular pathway, defining its essential components, order of operations, and decisive triggering events.

ideas.title: An iterative, data-constrained modeling and experimental pipeline for TGN sorting mechanisms
ideas.description: This project establishes a tightly coupled cycle of computational modeling and targeted experimentation to overcome the critical data gap in lipid-protein interaction networks at the TGN. A preliminary computational framework identifies the most impactful unknown parameters, such as local lipid concentrations and binding kinetics, guiding the design of focused lipidomic profiling and quantitative live-cell imaging assays in specific cell models. The resulting high-resolution data is then used to iteratively refine and validate the model's predictions. This closed-loop approach ensures the generation of a robust, experimentally grounded model that can make accurate and testable predictions about cargo sorting outcomes based on defined lipid-protein interaction states.

ideas.title: Spatially-resolved TGN lipidomics via proximity-dependent biotinylation and quantitative mass spectrometry
ideas.description: This approach employs a genetically encoded APEX2 peroxidase fused to a resident TGN protein to enzymatically biotinylate lipids and proteins exclusively within the TGN lumen and immediate membranes in living cells. Following cell lysis under denaturing conditions, biotinylated lipids are captured using streptavidin beads with high specificity, separating them from the bulk cellular lipidome. The isolated lipids are then analyzed by targeted and untargeted liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry to generate a quantitative, spatially resolved lipid map of the native TGN environment. This system directly overcomes the contamination problem of classical fractionation, enabling the correlation of specific lipid species with distinct TGN sorting functions and cargoes.

ideas.title: Adapting a Neurotransmitter Sniffer Strategy for Multi-Lipid Imaging at the TGN
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from neuroscience, where 'sniffer' biosensors detect transient neurotransmitter release, to the problem of monitoring multiple lipid species at the TGN. The analogous solution uses receptor-based domains tethered to a membrane to detect specific extracellular chemicals. Adapted to cell biology, we propose engineering a panel of lipid 'sniffer' biosensors. Each sensor would consist of a lipid-specific binding domain (e.g., for DAG, ceramide, or sphingosine-1-phosphate) fused to a circularly permuted fluorescent protein and anchored to the cytosolic leaflet of the TGN membrane via a specific targeting sequence (e.g., from TGN38). This creates a toolkit of intensity-based, single-fluorophore sensors enabling simultaneous, multi-color live-cell imaging of the spatial and temporal dynamics of several key signaling lipids at the TGN with minimal perturbation.

ideas.title: Functional exchange of TGN lipid machinery: Testing cross-kingdom complementation of a defined PI4K/sphingolipid pathway
ideas.description: This project tests the deep functional conservation of TGN lipid biology by asking if a specific lipid-modifying enzyme from plants can replace the function of its fungal ortholog, and vice versa. We focus on a key enzyme generating a defined TGN lipid signal (e.g., PtdIns4P) and use engineered mutant lines in a model fungus (e.g., *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) and plant (e.g., *Arabidopsis thaliana*) that are conditionally dependent on heterologous expression for survival or correct protein trafficking. By measuring rescue of growth, secretion, and specific lipid pool restoration, the work moves beyond sequence comparison to directly assay whether the core mechanistic role of the TGN lipid module is conserved across eukaryotic kingdoms.

ideas.title: Acute, compartment-specific lipid manipulation to dissect causal PM-to-TGN feedback in sorting
ideas.description: This study uses a chemogenetic toolkit to acutely and reversibly alter specific lipid levels (e.g., phosphatidylserine, phosphoinositides) exclusively at the plasma membrane (PM) via PM-anchored lipid-modifying enzymes controlled by chemically induced dimerization. The immediate impact of these spatially restricted lipid changes is measured using live-cell imaging of fluorescently tagged cargo (e.g., VSVG, E-cadherin) exiting the TGN, alongside biosensors for TGN membrane order and sorting machinery recruitment. This approach establishes direct causality by isolating PM lipid signaling from global metabolic feedback and defines the kinetic and molecular requirements for the retrograde signal.

ideas.title: Adapt proximity labeling to map lipid-proximal proteome
ideas.description: The original idea proposes using APEX or BioID fused to lipid-binding domains to map the proteome near lipids at the TGN. Drawing an analogy from marine biology, where sensor packages are deployed to sample the dynamic chemical environment around deep-sea hydrothermal vents, we adapt this solution. The analogous concept involves precise positioning of a sensor at a fluid-chemical interface for time-integrated capture of proximal components. Adapted to cell biology, this translates to engineering a lipid-specific proximity labeling sensor: a promiscuous biotin ligase (TurboID for faster kinetics) is fused to a high-affinity, well-characterized lipid-binding domain (e.g., a PI4P-specific PH domain). This molecular 'sensor' is targeted to the TGN. Upon brief induction of labeling, it biotinylates proteins that are transiently or stably proximal to the PI4P-rich membrane domains, enabling the systematic identification of the 'lipid-proximal proteome' for a specific lipid species, capturing effectors, regulators, and transporters that define the TGN's functional landscape.

ideas.title: Functional Nanodomain Mapping of TGN Lipids via Bioorthogonal Labeling and Correlative Super-Resolution Disruption
ideas.description: This redesigned approach addresses the critical flaw of labeling artifacts by employing metabolic incorporation of bioorthogonal alkyne-tagged lipid precursors (e.g., for sphingolipids and diacylglycerol analogs) into cellular pools, followed by gentle, post-fixation click conjugation to bright, photostable dyes for dSTORM imaging. To establish functional relevance, the experimental system includes acute, spatially controlled pharmacological or optogenetic perturbations designed to disrupt specific lipid interactions within the imaged TGN region. Correlative live-cell imaging of cargo export efficiency from the same TGN sub-region before fixation and super-resolution imaging directly tests whether the observed nanoscale lipid organizations are necessary for TGN sorting function.

ideas.title: Causally link defined TGN lipid perturbations to specific cargo sorting errors using orthogonal enzymatic control
ideas.description: This project establishes a direct causal chain from altered TGN lipid composition to disease-relevant cargo mistrafficking. Using optogenetic or chemically induced lipid-modifying enzymes (e.g., PI4K, sphingomyelin synthases) targeted to the TGN, we will create precise, acute lipid imbalances in otherwise healthy cells. The sorting of fluorescently tagged, disease-related cargos (e.g., progranulin, APP, GLUT4) will be tracked in real-time using high-content imaging and single-vesicle analysis. This system isolates lipid metabolism as the independent variable, directly testing its sufficiency to disrupt specific sorting pathways and identifying the exact trafficking steps most vulnerable to lipid dysregulation.

ideas.title: A High-Throughput, Barcoded Library Screen to Define the TGN Lipid-Protein Interactome
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from functional genomics to systematically map the lipid-protein interaction network at the trans-Golgi network (TGN). The analogous problem—unbiased mapping of all protein-protein interactions governing a specific cellular phenotype—was solved using high-throughput, barcoded library screens coupled with phenotypic selection and deep sequencing. Here, we propose to create a comprehensive, barcoded library of known and predicted lipid-binding domains and full-length TGN proteins. This library will be expressed in a engineered cellular system where correct cargo sorting at the TGN is linked to a robust, selectable output (e.g., cell survival or a fluorescent reporter signal). By applying selection pressure for functional cargo sorting and then performing deep sequencing to quantify barcode enrichment in the successfully sorted vesicle population versus the starting library, we can identify the complete set of lipid-binding modules and proteins that are functionally required for TGN-mediated sorting. This approach moves beyond static interaction catalogs to define the dynamically engaged lipid-protein interactome that governs cargo trafficking.

ideas.title: Spatiotemporal lipidomic mapping of TGN membrane dynamics to decode disease-specific cargo sorting failures
ideas.description: This project integrates high-resolution TGN lipidomics with functional cargo trafficking assays in disease models to establish causative links. By applying lipidomic profiling to isolated TGN membranes from cells under disease-relevant stress or with genetic perturbations, we will identify specific lipid imbalances that correlate with the mistrafficking of key disease-related cargos (e.g., progranulin, APP, or insulin receptors). The synergy lies in using the lipidomic data not just as a descriptive snapshot but to actively manipulate those specific lipid species in follow-up experiments, thereby testing their direct role in sorting machinery dysfunction and validating them as mechanistic drivers of pathology.

ideas.title: Quantifying the multi-scale lipid exchange dynamics that maintain the stable TGN landscape
ideas.description: This project moves beyond a static/dynamic binary to define the precise exchange kinetics that underlie the apparent stability of the TGN lipid landscape. Using a palette of spectrally distinct, environmentally sensitive lipid probes, we will perform simultaneous super-resolution imaging and quantitative fluorescence fluctuation spectroscopy on the intact TGN in living cells. This integrated approach will measure the persistence of mesoscale lipid domains over minutes, while also quantifying the millisecond-scale diffusion and exchange rates of lipids within and between these domains. The outcome will be a kinetic map defining whether TGN lipid organization is a frozen scaffold or a dynamically maintained steady state, and how these kinetics are perturbed by cargo sorting events.

ideas.title: A multi-compartment flux-balance model identifies lipid-dependent and lipid-independent bottlenecks in the secretory pathway
ideas.description: This project develops a whole-cell kinetic model of the secretory pathway that moves beyond a single-organelle focus. It integrates discrete lipid environments and enzymatic activities across all major secretory compartments—from ER exit to plasma membrane fusion—with the core protein machinery governing vesicle budding, transport, and fusion. The model is designed to simulate how perturbations to specific lipid species in different compartments affect global secretory flux, thereby distinguishing true lipid-dependent bottlenecks from steps limited by protein machinery or cargo availability. Validation employs systematic lipidomic and flux measurements in engineered yeast or mammalian cells with targeted lipid pathway disruptions, enabling the model to predict the phenotypic outcome of combinatorial perturbations and identify optimal targets for modulating secretion.

ideas.title: Correlative Nanoscale Mapping of TGN Lipid Domain Composition and Proximal Proteome Dynamics
ideas.description: This fused approach integrates lipid domain-specific proximity labeling (e.g., APEX2 fused to sphingolipid- or DAG-binding domains) with super-resolution microscopy (STORM/PALM) of the same lipid domains in the TGN of intact cells. By performing proximity biotinylation specifically from defined lipid-binding probes, followed by proteomic analysis, we identify the proteins enriched near specific lipid species. Crucially, concurrent super-resolution imaging of the lipid domains, using compatible labeling strategies, provides the nanoscale spatial context for where these proximal proteome interactions occur. This correlative map allows us to test if proteins identified by proximity labeling are selectively enriched at nanodomains of their cognate lipid, revealing the protein-lipid interactions that drive TGN sub-compartmentalization and vesicle formation.

ideas.title: Adapting Air Traffic Control Algorithms to Model and Correct Lipid-Based Cargo Sorting Errors at the TGN
ideas.description: This idea uses an analogy from Air Traffic Control (ATC) Systems Engineering to expand the original seed. In ATC, predictive algorithms integrate sensor data to model trajectories, detect conflicts from disruptions, and issue corrective instructions. We adapt this solution by proposing the development of a computational, predictive model for the Trans-Golgi Network (TGN). This model would integrate dynamic lipidomic profiles ('sensor data') with established rules of cargo-lipid interactions ('flight plans') to simulate and predict cargo sorting outcomes. Its core function would be to identify specific mistrafficking events predicted to result from disease-related lipid imbalances ('conflict detection'). Finally, the model would be used in silico to screen and propose targeted lipid-modifying interventions ('corrective routing vectors'), such as modulating specific lipid-metabolizing enzymes or administering defined lipid species, to restore normal cargo trafficking in neurodegenerative or metabolic diseases.

ideas.title: Spatially constrained minimal system: Reconstituting TGN lipid sorting with membrane tension and cortical actin mimics
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simple liposomes to create a synthetic membrane system that captures key physical and organizational features of the TGN. A patterned supported lipid bilayer defines distinct, non-mixing lipid domains analogous to TGN subdomains, into which sorting machinery is reconstituted. The system integrates with microfluidics to apply controlled membrane tension and to allow the sequential addition of minimal cytoskeletal elements, such as actin filaments and myosin motors. This enables a direct test of which physical and organizational constraints are essential for efficient, directional lipid sorting observed in vivo.

ideas.title: Comparative Lipidomics of the TGN: A Phylogenetic Signal-to-Noise Approach
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution from evolutionary linguistics, where the problem of distinguishing homologous traits (cognates) from analogous ones (false friends) is solved via phylogenetic comparative methods. We will analyze the conservation and divergence of TGN lipid functions across eukaryotic kingdoms (plants and fungi) by first building a robust phylogeny of the involved molecular machinery (enzymes, lipid transporters, effectors). This phylogenetic framework will then be used to perform a 'lipidomic signal-to-noise' analysis, explicitly separating deeply conserved, ancestral lipid roles (the homologous 'signal') from lineage-specific adaptations or convergent functions that arose independently (the analogous 'noise'). This approach will reveal whether observed functional similarities are due to shared descent or convergent evolution, providing a clearer evolutionary narrative for TGN lipid biology across kingdoms.

ideas.title: Adapting Network Control Theory from Ecology to Map Sphingolipid-DAG Crosstalk in Membrane Sorting
ideas.description: This research adapts solutions from theoretical ecology to elucidate cross-talk between sphingolipid and DAG pathways. In ecology, network control theory is used to model how perturbations cascade through and are regulated by feedback loops in complex food webs to determine community structure. By analogy, we will treat the sphingolipid and DAG signaling networks as a dynamic cellular 'interaction web.' The adapted idea involves using controlled perturbations (e.g., specific enzyme inhibitors, optogenetic DAG generation) combined with high-resolution lipidomics and live-cell biosensor imaging to quantitatively map the causal flow of information. This data will be used to construct a predictive mathematical model that identifies key feedback and feedforward loops, ultimately revealing how these two critical lipid networks integrate to coordinate membrane sorting decisions.

ideas.title: Spatiotemporally controlled synthetic cargoes for reversible TGN lipid domain engineering
ideas.description: This project designs synthetic cargo proteins whose ability to bind and reorganize specific TGN lipids is controlled by light or a small molecule, allowing precise activation and deactivation. These cargoes are screened using a microfluidic platform that replicates the TGN's lipid and protein composition to identify constructs that induce controlled, local lipid domain changes. The goal is to achieve reversible manipulation of lipid order and composition at a specific TGN subdomain, directing cargo sorting without global organelle dysfunction. This enables causal testing of how local lipid environments determine vesicle budding and protein export from the TGN.

ideas.title: Apply lipidomics to profile TGN membranes during sorting using a metabolic pulse-chase strategy
ideas.description: This idea adapts the pulse-chase framework of Stable Isotope Probing (SIP) from microbial ecology to study TGN lipid dynamics. In SIP, a heavy isotope pulse is tracked through a complex community to link metabolic function to specific members. Here, we apply this temporal tracking logic by pulse-labeling cultured cells with stable isotope-labeled lipid precursors (e.g., ¹³C-choline, ¹⁵N-serine). Following a chase, TGN membranes are purified via subcellular fractionation at defined time points corresponding to distinct sorting stages. Subsequent high-resolution mass spectrometry-based lipidomics will then quantify the incorporation and turnover of the labeled atoms into specific lipid classes (e.g., phosphoinositides, sphingomyelin). This approach transforms a static TGN lipidomic snapshot into a kinetic profile, directly revealing how lipid composition and flux correlate with the progression of cargo sorting through the organelle.

ideas.title: Adapting Fluorescent Voltage-Sensing Techniques to Map Lipid Potential Gradients During Vesicle Transport
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from neurophysiology, where genetically encoded fluorescent voltage indicators enable optical recording of rapid electrical potential changes in neurons. The analogous problem in lipid biology is measuring rapid, localized changes in lipid concentration or membrane organization during vesicular transport. The adapted solution is to create a new class of biosensors: lipid-specific binding domains coupled to environmentally sensitive fluorescent proteins, targeted to specific organelle membranes. These 'lipid potentiometers' would allow quantification of spatiotemporal lipid dynamics (e.g., sphingolipid and DAG levels) with high resolution during specific cargo trafficking events, optically reporting lipid fluxes as vesicles form, move, and fuse, analogous to how voltage sensors report electrical fluxes during an action potential.
